project.name: rowshape
project.tagline: The type-checker for database migrations — a human and an agent get the same answer through the same contract.
project.one_liner: Execute a proposed schema change against production-shaped data in a disposable environment and return a machine-readable verdict.

license.rationale: PRD §8.4 — the moat is being the default in the agent loop; every restriction taxes exactly that. MIT chosen, not defaulted.

cli.rationale: PRD §7 — single static binary no runtime; native dialect of the category; hydrate is CPU-bound; advantages compound over time.
explicitly_no: gofakeit / general fake-data library (own synthesis engine — PRD §16)

marketing_and_docs.rationale: PRD §9 — fits existing Pages standard, purpose-built for docs+landing, ships zero JS by default. The Go decision was about the binary, not the website.

api.rationale: PRD §9 — RFC §11 canonical form must have exactly ONE implementation. If the server recomputes canonicalization in TypeScript, digests eventually disagree and attestations silently stop verifying. Not Deno edge functions for this reason.
api.phase: 5 (recorded now, built later; constrains v1 verdict shape via §9.1)

datastore.service: self-hosted Supabase (on Coolify, alongside the API)
datastore.rationale: PRD §9 — single VPS is defensible because validate never calls the cloud, fixtures are committed to git (registry is convenience not dependency), and fixtures are value-free by design so a breach exposes statistics not customer data.

loop.description: This file is the single source of truth for the build loop (Claude /loop). The work signal is `passes`: every story with `passes: false` is unfinished and is work to do. Each iteration: read this file, select the next actionable `passes: false` story, do exactly that story, verify it, flip `passes` to true only when it truly passes, commit. Repeat until no `passes: false` stories remain.
loop.work_signal: passes (boolean). false = needs work, true = done and verified. To list all remaining work: every story where passes == false.
operating_rules: Study the referenced spec section(s) in `spec_refs` BEFORE implementing a task. The spec wins over intuition.
Do exactly ONE story per iteration. Do not batch or skip ahead.
A story may only be selected when every id in its `depends_on` has `passes: true`.
Never set `passes` to true without every `acceptance_criteria` met AND every `verification` command passing.
Never weaken an invariant, downgrade a confidence level, or relax an acceptance criterion to make a task pass. If blocked, set status `blocked` and record why.
Respect all entries in `invariants` on every task, in every phase, forever.
Prefer the smallest change that satisfies the task. No speculative surface area.
After each task: commit with the task id in the message, then re-read this file before selecting the next.
Check `kill_criteria` at the named checkpoints; if a threshold is hit, stop and surface it rather than pushing on.
loop.task_selection: Lowest phase order first; within a phase, lowest `priority` number among stories with `passes: false` whose `depends_on` are all `passes: true`.
loop.status_note: `status` is optional human context (use `blocked` to record a stuck story). `passes` is the authoritative done-signal the loop reads.
stop_conditions: No `passes: false` stories remain in any non-deferred phase.
A milestone marked as the launch gate (see `milestones`) is satisfied.
A kill_criteria threshold is triggered (stop and surface, do not auto-continue).

invariants.statement: The verdict is one Go `Verdict` struct with two marshalers (JSON + human). Human output is always a rendering of the JSON, never a separate code path. CLI, MCP server, and GitHub Action all render the same struct.

The verdict contract (fields, codes, exit codes) is the public API. Finding codes are permanent and namespaced (RS-LOCK, RS-DATA, RS-CONSTRAINT, RS-INDEX, RS-PERF, RS-REVERSE). Exit codes: 0 PASS, 1 FAIL, 2 WARN-only, 3 tool error. `remediation` is mandatory on every error.

A verdict is capped by the minimum confidence across the facts it relied on. exact/measured -> PASS allowed; estimated/declared/absent -> WARN max. A finding resting on estimated facts CANNOT produce PASS; it produces WARN naming the command that resolves it. Findings MUST NOT downgrade a dependency's confidence to reach a stronger verdict.
invariants.priority: This is the one thing that must never be wrong (a wrong PASS is unrecoverable).

invariants.statement: `unique` MUST be `exact` or absent. Uniqueness is NEVER inferred from a sample.

Durations are reported as buckets (instant/fast/noticeable/slow/outage) with the extrapolation basis attached, never as point estimates. Extrapolation is model-based (per operation class) and engine-version-conditional; refuse to extrapolate when engine.version is absent.

A fixture contains no rows — only statistics. Never emit `range`/min/max on text or bytea columns. `strict` privacy must be supported; emitters MUST NOT default to `permissive`. `inspect --leaks` must enumerate every value-derived field.

Canonical form (keys sorted lexicographically, two-space indent, no anchors/aliases, floats to 6 sig figs, \n line endings, meta.digest + meta.generated_at excluded) has exactly ONE implementation, in Go, shared by CLI and API.

Verdicts are emitted in a DSSE/in-toto-signable predicate shape from day one (subject = fixture digest + migration file digests; predicateType = https://rowshape.com/attestation/v1; predicate = verdict body incl depends_on + confidence). No signing infra in v1 — just a format that won't need retrofitting.
`validate` is free forever and never calls the cloud. The moment `validate` is gated, the agent loop breaks and the wedge dies.

No `rowshape apply` in v1. `validate` never touches a non-disposable DB (hard-refuse if target host matches the fixture source host). `verify` is read-only. `pull` requires a read-only role; refuse superuser without --i-know.
invariants.implementation_note: 'Matches' means the same MACHINE, not the same string — the distinction was not academic. The refusal was bypassable and proven so on a live database: a fixture pulled from `localhost`, validated with `--ephemeral` against `127.0.0.1`, went straight past CheckHost and began creating a database on the very server the fixture came from. `DB.Internal` vs `db.internal` (DNS is case-insensitive, RFC 4343) and a trailing FQDN dot did the same. Closed in two halves, both required: profile.HashSource now NORMALIZES the host before hashing (lowercase, no trailing dot, no IPv6 brackets) — that half must be at emit time because a hash cannot be inverted, so if the odd spelling is the one already in meta.source, nothing at check time can recover it; and validate.CheckHost hashes the target under every spelling that is definitionally the same machine (verbatim, normalized, and all loopback aliases incl. 127.0.0.0/8, ::1), refusing on any match — which normalization alone cannot do, since `localhost` and `127.0.0.1` each normalize to themselves and are still one host. Covered by 13 table cases in internal/validate (both directions of every equivalence, plus negative cases: a different host and 128.0.0.1 must still be ALLOWED, or validate refuses to do its job). Mutation-tested: reverting to the single-hash compare fails with the exact bypass. KNOWN LIMIT, deliberate: DNS is not resolved, so `db.internal` and its IP are still not recognized as one host. That needs a network call from inside a safety check and an answer that can change between check and connect. This refusal is the last line, not the only one — point --ephemeral at a genuinely disposable server, not a hostname that merely looks different from production.
invariants.verified: Live: the exact bypass now exits 3 with the refusal; a genuinely different host still proceeds.

invariants.statement: hydrate --seed N produces identical output for the same fixture + hydrator version across platforms. Per-column PRNG derived via hash(seed, table, column, row_ordinal); generation order must not depend on Go map iteration order (canonicalize first).
Ship SBOM and cosign signatures from the first release. Single static binary, no runtime, deps kept deliberately few.

kill_criteria.test: If pull + hydrate don't produce a database that reproduces a known corpus pathology, the fixture premise is wrong. Stop before building validate on top of it.
If the agent-loop demo doesn't work end-to-end without a human turn, the wedge isn't real. Reassess whether a 3rd linter in this market is worth continuing.
If there are zero unsolicited issues or PRs from people you didn't tell about it, that's information — do not push harder on momentum alone.

tasks.namespace_check: NAMESPACE AVAILABILITY CHECKED (not assumed) — the single biggest assumption in the repo is that `rowshape` is free, since go.mod, every import path, the goreleaser config, the npm wrapper and the docs all hardcode it. If any of it were taken this is not a blocked story, it is a redesign. Findings: (1) rowshape.com IS REGISTERED — it returns NOERROR with no A record and is delegated to Cloudflare nameservers (wesley.ns.cloudflare.com / val.ns.cloudflare.com), where an unregistered domain returns NXDOMAIN (verified against a control). Cloudflare matches PRD §9's Cloudflare Pages choice, so this is very likely already the owner's and criterion 1 is MET — worth confirming it is yours rather than a squatter's, but the evidence is strong. It has no A record yet, consistent with registered-but-not-yet-deployed. (2) The `rowshape` GitHub org is FREE (users/rowshape -> 404), as are rowshape/rowshape, rowshape/fixture-spec, rowshape/corpus and rowshape/homebrew-tap (the tap the goreleaser cask targets). (3) The npm package name `rowshape` is FREE (registry 404). So the plan is NOT compromised: the two remaining reservations are still available, and nothing built so far rests on a name someone else holds. Re-check before acting — availability is a race, not a fact.
tasks.blocked_reason: Automatable part done: canonical Go module path github.com/rowshape/rowshape decided and recorded in docs/DECISIONS.md (D-001). The three external reservations (register rowshape.com, create the `rowshape` GitHub org, reserve the `rowshape` npm name) require owner action and cannot be performed from this environment; tracked as a checklist in docs/DECISIONS.md D-002. Module path is settled, so downstream code tasks (P0-T3+) are unblocked.

tasks.found_by_audit: The conformance suite's promise was uncallable. Its package doc says a third party "can run the same suite against their own implementation — this is what makes the spec a position rather than an aspiration (PRD §3, §16)", and every exported check took *fixture.Fixture — a type in rowshape's INTERNAL tree. Go forbids importing .../internal/... across module boundaries, so no outside module could name the argument, let alone build one. PROVEN both ways with a scratch module: importing internal/fixture fails to compile ("use of internal package github.com/rowshape/rowshape/internal/fixture not allowed"), and after the fix an external module calls conformance.CheckEmitterYAML on its own bytes and gets back the real violation ([§6.1 no range on text]). This matters because PRD §3 makes the spec the POSITION: "anyone can emit it" is the strategic claim, and the executable suite backing it could only ever be run by rowshape itself. FIXED minimally: CheckEmitterYAML([]byte) takes bytes — what an emitter actually has — and returns violations, with a parse failure as an error rather than a Violation (unreadable bytes are the ABSENCE of a verdict, not a passing one). DOCS CORRECTED to stop overclaiming: CheckHydrator/CheckValidator call hydrate.Generate and the verdict engine directly, so they are regression tests that the REFERENCE implementation obeys its own spec — not a harness a third party can plug a hydrator into, which would need an agreed wire format for hydrated rows that RFC-0001 does not define. TWO NOTES FOR THIS STORY WHEN IT UNBLOCKS: (1) the Go suite CANNOT move to rowshape/fixture-spec as-is — it imports internal/{fixture,estimate,hydrate,verdict}, which stop compiling the moment it is a separate module; the spec repo should carry RFC-0001.md + README + LICENSE + schema/, with the Go suite staying here (or the model promoted out of internal/). (2) This story's named artifacts fixture-spec/RFC-0001.md, README.md and LICENSE do not exist — the RFC lives at the repo root as RFC-0001-rowshape-fixture-spec.md. AUDITED CLEAN in the same pass: conformance.yml genuinely installs check-jsonschema and validates every reference AND corpus fixture against the published schema, with a negative case asserting a non-conformant fixture is rejected — the suite is real, not decorative.

tasks.title: goreleaser release pipeline across all channels
tasks.description: Configure goreleaser to build darwin/linux/windows on amd64+arm64 and publish to GitHub Releases, a Homebrew tap, `go install`, an npm wrapper package that fetches the platform binary on install (so `npx rowshape` works), and a FROM scratch Docker image for CI.

tasks.found_by_audit_2: THERE WAS NO RELEASE WORKFLOW. .goreleaser.yaml was valid and complete and nothing ran it — goreleaser was referenced by zero workflows. This story's note framed it as 'config complete, blocked on the org', which understated it: with the org reserved and a tag pushed, NOTHING would have happened. No binaries, no cask, no image. P0-T5 was worse off — cosign keyless signing needs a CI OIDC identity and syft must be installed on the runner, so with no workflow there could be no SBOM and no signature ever, on any release. ADDED .github/workflows/release.yml: tag v* (or manual dispatch for a publish-nothing dry run), tests before publishing, QEMU+buildx and ghcr login for the multi-arch dockers_v2 image, syft and cosign installed explicitly (goreleaser only orchestrates them; a missing tool fails the tag, and a tag is not cleanly retryable), id-token: write for keyless signing, and HOMEBREW_TAP_GITHUB_TOKEN — without which the release SUCCEEDS and the tap silently does not update, since GITHUB_TOKEN cannot write to rowshape/homebrew-tap. SECOND GAP, same area: npm publishing was automated nowhere, and npm/package.json ships version 0.0.0 while install.js builds its download URL from that version — publishing as-is would fetch releases/download/v0.0.0/ and 404 on every machine even with the naming fixed. The npm job now stamps the version from the tag, runs the naming check, and publishes after the release exists. Verified by simulation: stamping 1.2.3 makes install.js request v1.2.3/rowshape_1.2.3_darwin_amd64.tar.gz, which is exactly what goreleaser publishes for tag v1.2.3. STILL OWNER-GATED: the workflow needs secrets NPM_TOKEN and HOMEBREW_TAP_GITHUB_TOKEN, and the rowshape org must exist. RECORDED, NOT FIXED (needs a decision): the Dockerfile is FROM scratch with no CA certificates, so `rowshape pull` from the image against a managed Postgres with sslmode=verify-full would fail with x509: certificate signed by unknown authority. Fine for the stated CI use (a disposable DB over plain TCP), wrong for anything else. Options: bundle ca-certificates into the docker context, or use gcr.io/distroless/static (CA certs + tzdata) instead of scratch — which PRD §7 explicitly says. Untestable here (no Docker daemon). ALSO: the signs block covers `artifacts: checksum` only, so the checksums file is signed rather than each binary; that is goreleaser's recommended pattern and a complete verification chain, but P0-T5 criterion 1 says 'Each released binary has a cosign signature' — either set artifacts: all or reword the criterion.
tasks.found_by_audit: The npm wrapper asked for a file the release does not publish — every `npx rowshape` would have 404'd. install.js used the OLDER goreleaser convention (title-cased OS, amd64 rewritten to x86_64) and built `rowshape_1.0.0_Darwin_x86_64.tar.gz`, while .goreleaser.yaml's name_template is `{{ .ProjectName }}_{{ .Version }}_{{ .Os }}_{{ .Arch }}` — raw lowercase GOOS/GOARCH — publishing `rowshape_1.0.0_darwin_amd64.tar.gz`. PROVEN, not inferred: `goreleaser release --snapshot` emits rowshape_..._darwin_amd64.tar.gz / _linux_arm64.tar.gz / _windows_amd64.zip. Two files in different languages had to agree on a string and nothing compared them, so the mismatch could only surface at the first real release, inside a postinstall hook, on a user machine — exactly the acceptance criterion (`npx rowshape --help`) this story records as unverifiable without a release. FIXED: the maps emit lowercase GOOS/GOARCH; windows/arm64 (which goreleaser ignores, so no asset exists) now fails with that message rather than a confusing 404. install.js was also untestable — it downloaded on require — so the naming is an exported assetName() and side effects are guarded by require.main === module. npm/naming.test.js checks all 5 combos against the convention AND, when a dist/ build is present, against the archives goreleaser ACTUALLY produced. Mutation-proven: restoring the old naming fails it. This story's other claims were also re-verified first-hand: `goreleaser check` clean, `goreleaser release --snapshot` builds all 5 platform/arch combos.
tasks.blocked_reason: Config complete and locally verified: `goreleaser check` clean (no deprecations), `goreleaser release --snapshot` builds all 5 platform/arch combos (darwin/linux amd64+arm64, windows amd64), injects version via ldflags, and generates archives, checksums, and the Homebrew cask. Artifacts written: .goreleaser.yaml (builds/archives/homebrew_casks/dockers_v2/release), Dockerfile (FROM scratch), npm/ wrapper (install.js fetch-on-install + bin shim). Two acceptance criteria cannot be verified from this environment: `npx rowshape --help` requires a published GitHub Release for the wrapper to download, and `docker run <image> --help` requires a running Docker daemon (currently down). Both are release-time checks gated on the `rowshape` GitHub org + CI secrets (see P0-T1 / docs/DECISIONS.md D-002).

tasks.title: CI skeleton (build, test, lint)
tasks.description: GitHub Actions workflow that builds, runs `go test ./...`, and lints (gofmt/vet/golangci-lint) on every push and PR. This is the harness the corpus and later phases plug into.
acceptance_criteria: CI runs on push and PR.
Build + test + lint jobs are green on the skeleton.
Workflow is matrix-ready for the PG 11–17 version matrix added in phase 2.
verification: CI green on a trivial PR
tasks.note:
CORRECTED: this story previously claimed passes:true with the note 'golangci-lint run (0 issues)'. That was FALSE. golangci-lint reported 25 issues on main (18 errcheck, 6 staticcheck, 1 unused) and CI invoked golangci-lint-action@v6 with `version: latest` and no config file, so the lint job would have failed on first push — i.e. the acceptance criterion 'Build + test + lint jobs are green on the skeleton' was not met. The likely cause of the bad claim: golangci-lint v1 shipped default exclusions (EXC0001 et al) that v2 removed, so an earlier local run with a v1 binary genuinely printed 0 issues. Now actually satisfied.

Two REAL BUGS were hiding in the noise, both found by taking errcheck seriously instead of suppressing it:
(1) cmd/hydrate.go used `defer file.Close()` on a WRITE path. A failed flush (full disk, network filesystem) was silently dropped and hydrate returned success over a truncated SQL file — a silent wrong-success in the one command whose promise is byte-reproducible output. Now closed explicitly and checked, with the write-error path closing before returning.
(2) internal/plan.RedactURL leaked credentials. It cut at the FIRST '@', so a password containing an unencoded '@' (legal, and people write them) left its tail in displayed output: postgres://admin:p@ss@host/db -> postgres://…@ss@host/db. This directly violates PRD §5 (credentials never logged/persisted). Now cuts at the last '@' bounded to the authority, so an '@' in a path/query is not mistaken for a separator either. Found because the ONLY test touching redaction was a vacuous empty branch (staticcheck SA9003) that guarded on the URL containing '@' while running against a localhost URL — it could never fire, and RedactURL had no direct test at all. Replaced with internal/plan/redact_test.go (table cases + a property test asserting the secret never survives).

Lint config (.golangci.yml) is deliberately narrow, NOT a blanket suppression: errcheck excludes only fmt.Fprint/Fprintf/Fprintln (writes to stdout/stderr; the exit code is the contract, PRD §10) and hash.Hash.Write (documented never to error). Close is deliberately NOT excluded — excluding it would have hidden bug (1). Proven by mutation: reintroducing `defer file.Close()` in hydrate.go makes the config report the error again. The ~14 genuinely-safe deferred Close/Rollback sites say `_ =` at the call instead — a visible, reviewable claim per site. staticcheck: QF1001 (De Morgan) disabled with rationale — every site is a `!(runes[i]=='*' && runes[i+1]=='/')` scanner loop that reads as 'until the closing */'; the De Morgan form is equivalent and harder to check. All other staticcheck checks stay on; QF1003 (tagged switch) and S1011 were APPLIED rather than suppressed, and dead phase-0 code (cmd/root.go notImplemented, unused once every subcommand shipped) was deleted.

CI hardening: golangci-lint-action bumped v6 -> v8 (verified: v6 drives only golangci-lint v1 and CANNOT read a `version: "2"` config, so the config alone would have left CI broken in a new way) and the linter version pinned to v2.12.2 instead of `latest`, since `latest` turns CI red overnight on a commit that changed nothing and trains everyone to ignore the lint job. Workflow still runs on push + PR, matrix-ready via matrix.pg (single '16' entry, expansion documented in P2-T13). Verified with the exact CI command set: go build ./... , go test ./... (all green), gofmt -l (clean), go vet ./... (clean), golangci-lint run (0 issues).

phases.name: pull / hydrate for Postgres (deterministic, own synthesis engine)
phases.ships: `pull` (fast mode) and `hydrate` for Postgres. Reads production shape read-only, emits a committable rowshape.yaml, and reconstructs a deterministic disposable database from it.
phases.scope_note: Fast mode only: catalog + pg_stats + reservoir sample. HyperLogLog, uniqueness existence probes, auto-escalation, and `inspect --leaks` are DEFERRED to phase-1b. In phase 1, `distinct` stays `estimated` (via pg_stats) and `unique` is `exact` only when a catalog constraint/index proves it, otherwise absent (INV-UNIQUENESS).
definition_of_done: A read-only `pull` against a Postgres produces a valid, digest-stable, <100KB rowshape.yaml in fast mode, with a confidence attached to every fact and privacy enforced.
`hydrate --seed N` deterministically reconstructs a disposable database honoring null_fraction (+/-0.5%), `unique`, and fan-out distribution shape.
Declared rows and hydrated rows are never conflated (RFC §9).
Week-6 kill-criteria smoke passes: pull + hydrate reproduces a known migration pathology (kill_criteria Week 6).

tasks.title: Fixture Go data model (internal/fixture)
tasks.description: Model the entire RFC §5/§6 document as Go structs: meta (id, generated_at, generator, engine{name,version}, privacy, source, profile{mode,scanned_at,escalated}, digest); tables map keyed by qualified name; column profiles (universal §6.1, numeric/temporal §6.2, format classes §6.3); constraints §6.4; indexes §6.5; references/fanout §6.6. Scalar facts are {value, confidence, via} objects, NOT bare scalars. Readers MAY accept a bare scalar as shorthand for confidence:estimated (weakest reading). Unknown fields ignored, x_ vendor extensions preserved, unknown major version refused (RFC §12).
acceptance_criteria: Structs round-trip to/from the YAML examples in RFC §5 and §6 byte-for-byte (modulo canonicalization).
A bare scalar deserializes as {value, confidence: estimated} (RFC §6.1).
`tables` is a map keyed by qualified name, not a list (RFC §5).
Unknown fields are ignored; unknown major `rowshape_fixture` version is refused (RFC §12).
Confidence type enforces ordering exact > measured > estimated > declared (RFC §7.1).
verification: go test ./internal/fixture/... (round-trip + confidence-ordering tests)

tasks.title: Canonical form + digest (single Go implementation)
tasks.description: Implement RFC §11 canonical form: keys sorted lexicographically, two-space indent, no anchors/aliases, floats to 6 significant figures, \n line endings, meta.digest and meta.generated_at excluded. Compute meta.digest = SHA-256 over the canonical form. This is the ONE canonical implementation the phase-5 API will import (INV-ONE-CANONICAL-FORM).
acceptance_criteria: Two canonicalizations of the same fixture are byte-identical.
Digest is stable across runs against an unchanged database and across platforms (RFC §13).
Floats render to exactly 6 significant figures; keys are lexicographically sorted; meta.digest and meta.generated_at are excluded from the hashed bytes.
verification: go test ./internal/fixture/... (golden canonical-form + digest-stability tests, run on linux+darwin+windows in CI)

tasks.title: `pull` — read structure via catalog (read-only)
tasks.description: Implement schema reads via catalog queries only: tables, columns, types, structural nullability, constraints (§6.4), indexes (§6.5), references + on_delete (§6.6). No SELECT * on user tables. Require a read-only role; refuse to run as superuser without --i-know. An existing unique constraint/index is a proof of uniqueness -> set unique {value:true, confidence:exact, via:constraint} for free. Preserve validated:false (NOT VALID) constraints.
acceptance_criteria: Produces the tables map with constraints, indexes, and references populated from the catalog.
Unique constraint/index sets unique.confidence=exact via constraint (RFC §6.4).
validated:false constraints are preserved (RFC §6.4).
Refuses to run as superuser without --i-know; uses a read-only path (no writes, no SELECT * on user tables).
The connection URL and any credentials are never logged, persisted, or written into the fixture (PRD §5, Priya is the buyer).
verification: go test against a testcontainers Postgres with a seeded schema
assert superuser refusal without --i-know

tasks.title: `pull` fast-mode column profiling (pg_stats + reservoir sample)
tasks.description: Profile each column in fast mode: null_fraction, distinct (estimated via pg_stats), numeric/temporal range + optional histogram (§6.2), text length stats, and format-class inference from a sample against the closed vocabulary (§6.3) with `opaque` as mandatory fallback. MUST NOT emit range/min/max on text or bytea columns (§6.1) — only length. Most facts land at `estimated`. Uniqueness is NOT inferred here (that is catalog-proven in P1-T3 or deferred to phase-1b probes); never infer unique:true from the sample (INV-UNIQUENESS).
acceptance_criteria: Every emitted fact carries a confidence; sample-derived facts are `estimated`.
text and bytea columns NEVER receive range/min/max (only length) — asserted by test (RFC §6.1 MUST).
format is one of the closed §6.3 vocabulary; ambiguous columns emit `opaque`.
jsonb_shape carries a key skeleton (names/depth/leaf types) but never leaf values (§6.3).
No unique:true is produced from the sample.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (text/bytea range-omission, format-class, jsonb skeleton, confidence-tagging)

tasks.title: Privacy levels + redaction in the emitter
tasks.description: Enforce RFC §8 at emit time. strict / standard (default) / permissive. strict emits none of: numeric/temporal range, histograms, values/frequencies, verbatim CHECK expressions (they become opaque). permissive materializes values/frequencies only when distinct<=50 AND every value occurs >= k times (default k=20). Per-column overrides (redact: [range, histogram] | all) always win. MUST support strict; MUST NOT default to permissive. meta.source is a salted per-fixture hash of the host, never the hostname (§8.4).
acceptance_criteria: strict output contains no range, histogram, values/frequencies, or verbatim CHECK expressions.
default privacy is `standard`; permissive is never the default.
permissive value sets appear only when distinct<=50 AND min per-value count >= k (k=20 default).
per-column `redact` overrides are honored.
meta.source is a salted hash, never the hostname.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (per-level field matrix from RFC §8.2, k-threshold, redact overrides, source-hash)

tasks.found_by_audit: meta.digest was computed, stored, and NEVER CHECKED — and that enabled a wrong PASS. This story's verification line says "rowshape (re)digest matches", but no command verified it and VerifyDigest, though exported, was called by nothing. DEMONSTRATED against a live database: editing a pulled fixture to read null_fraction {value: 0.0, confidence: exact} made `validate` return PASS, exit 0, for a SET NOT NULL against a column that is 2.9% null in production — while the stale digest sat in the file claiming the content was untouched. That is the wrong PASS INV-CONFIDENCE-CAPPING calls unrecoverable, reachable by editing a text file, with the mechanism built to detect it present and unenforced. Worth recording that capping caught the HALF-tamper: changing only the value to a bare scalar reads as `estimated`, which can never license PASS, so it returned WARN — the invariant did its job. Forging the confidence too defeated it, which is exactly what the digest exists for. FIXED: fixture.ParseVerified refuses a fixture whose stored digest does not match its content, and all five CLI load points (validate, hydrate, inspect, verify, and the MCP describe_shape path) use it. A fixture with NO digest is accepted — every one of the 30 fixtures in this repo's corpus and test suites is hand-authored and carries none, and demanding one would mean rowshape only ever reads its own output. The refusal prints both digests and says to re-run `rowshape pull` or delete meta.digest when hand-authoring. Verified live: the tampered fixture now exits 3 where it returned PASS; the untampered pull and the no-digest fixtures still load. Mutation-proven. This matters beyond tampering — a merge resolution or a YAML-rewriting tool staleness the digest silently, and it is the subject of every attestation (INV-DSSE-SHAPE), so an attestation over a fixture whose digest does not match its content asserts nothing.
tasks.title: Emit rowshape.yaml (assembled fixture)
tasks.description: Assemble the profiled data into a rowshape.yaml with a complete meta block: id, generated_at, generator, engine{name,version} (version MANDATORY — RFC §9.1), privacy, source (salted hash), profile{mode:fast, scanned_at, escalated:[]}, and computed digest. Write human-readable, diffable YAML. `rowshape init` should scaffold config; wire the minimal `init` here.
acceptance_criteria: Output is valid YAML that round-trips through the P1-T1 data model.
meta.engine.version is always present.
Fixture for a 200-table schema is under 100KB (RFC §3.3).
meta.digest matches a fresh recomputation over the file.
verification: rowshape pull $TEST_URL -> rowshape.yaml (connection URL is POSITIONAL; there is no --from flag — the original text here said `pull --from $TEST_URL`, which cannot ever have been run: it exits 3); rowshape (re)digest matches; yaml lint; size check on a synthetic 200-table schema

tasks.known_defect_orphan: FIXED. hydrate invented orphan foreign keys — the exact condition the fixture proves absent. parentIDValue returned the parent ORDINAL, on the reasoning (in its own comment) that 'a unique numeric id with no range is generated as its ordinal'. The caveat was the bug: a real `pull` emits a range for every numeric column (RFC §6.2), so a real fixture's id column HAS one, and numericInRange generates min + (ordinal % span) — for range {1,5000} that is 1..N, not 0..N-1. Children therefore referenced 0..N-1 while parents were 1..N, and user_id=0 pointed at nothing. Proven on the real pulled fixture at --scale 0.001: users.id=[1..5], user_id=0..4, 1 orphan of 20 — against a declared orphan_fraction {value: 0, confidence: exact, via: constraint}. Consequence: a migration adding FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id) fails on hydrated data and rowshape reports a FAIL IT MANUFACTURED — a false FAIL from the one tool whose verdicts are meant to be reproductions. parentIDValue now derives the value from the referenced parent column (parentIDColumn resolves ref.To) and runs the same generator over the same ordinal, so the two stay in step whatever the range is; it falls back to the ordinal only when the fixture carries no facts for that column, which is what the id would be in that case anyway. Verified live: user_id now 1..5 against users.id 1..5, zero orphans. TEST NOTE: the two hydrate defects INTERACT, and the first version of this test was vacuous because of it — with a fan-out fact, scaledTargetCounts (see known_defect_fanout) zeroes the low quantiles, so parent ordinal 0 is never handed out and the off-by-one cannot appear. The test now covers the uniform spread (no fan-out fact, out[i] = i % parentN, which always uses ordinal 0) as well; under mutation that case reports 4 of 400 orphans at user_id=0 while the fan-out case still passes — the masking, demonstrated.
tasks.known_defect_orphans: FIXED — and my reason for deferring it was wrong. I recorded this as over-constrained ("reproducing orphan_fraction and the fan-out's p50/p95/max simultaneously is over-constrained by the facts a fixture carries"). It is not. `pull` measures fan-out with GROUP BY on the foreign key (internal/profile/fanout.go), so its "parents" are FK VALUES, not parent rows — an orphan group is simply a value with no match. The two facts describe different things and never conflict: group sizes stay exactly as targetCounts built them, and what changes is only how many real parents end up childless, which the facts already imply. Arithmetic on the corpus fixture: 800000 children / mean 8 = 100000 distinct FK values; 0.4% = 3200 orphan rows; so ~3200 values are orphans, 96800 parents keep children, 3200 go childless — consistent throughout. IMPLEMENTATION: orphanGroupCount takes groups from the BOTTOM of the ascending counts (orphans in real data are stragglers, not whales, and taking from the bottom leaves p50/p95/max where they were) until their rows reach childN*orphan_fraction, and hands them ordinals >= parentN. parentIDValue maps those to maxParentID+1+j — beyond the largest id generated — which is the only choice that holds whatever the column's range is: min + (ordinal % span) wraps, so merely picking an unused ordinal would land on a real parent when the span is narrower than the parent count, and the orphan would quietly become valid. It never orphans every group. VERIFIED on the corpus's own validate_orphans fixture: declared 0.004 -> hydrated 0.0040 (3200 of 800000), fan-out mean/max unchanged at 8.00/900. Mutation-proven both ways, including the reverse (a fixture proving orphan_fraction 0 exact must hydrate clean, or ADD FOREIGN KEY fails on data rowshape itself invented). ISOLATED, NOT ASSUMED: measured the distribution with and without the change and it is byte-identical, so the orphan work does not disturb the fan-out. RESIDUAL, SEPARATE: that measurement also showed this fixture hydrates p50 1 against a declared 3 — a pre-existing accuracy limit of the fan-out model, not caused by this fix. Its shape (p95 40 is 13x p50 3) implies a mean near 9.75 against a declared 8, so reconcile removes ~175k rows from the body and pushes it toward 1. Four quantiles cannot determine a distribution and any synthesis interpolates; the model is exact on the real pulled fixture (p50 2, p95 4, max 7942) and close on the Week-6 gate's (p50 4 vs 5), and worst when p95 greatly exceeds p50. Recorded rather than chased: it is an approximation quality question, not a correctness bug.
tasks.known_defect_fanout: FIXED. The moat field was not reproduced at realistic skew. Found by pulling a REAL database (5k users, 20k orders, one whale owning 40% of orders) and hydrating it back: declared mean 4.09 / p50 2 / p95 4 / max 7942 hydrated as mean 4.00 / p50 0 / p95 0 / max 1244, with 95% of parents (4767 of 5000) getting ZERO children. Only the mean survived — exactly what P1-T7 says is not enough, on the field RFC/PRD §6.6 call the one no other tool captures. ROOT CAUSE: the model read far more into the facts than they say. It interpolated p95 -> max across the whole top 5% of parents, i.e. assumed 5% were near-whales; with max 7942 that band alone claimed ~1M children against childN 20k, so normalising multiplied everything by ~0.02 and rounded every typical parent from 2 to 0. KEY INSIGHT that made this decidable rather than a judgment call: `pull` measures fan-out with GROUP BY on the foreign key (internal/profile/fanout.go), so the declared facts describe only parents that HAVE children. That inverts cleanly — nonEmpty = childN/mean recovers 4894 of 5000, exactly production's figure — and it means the facts say NOTHING about the 96th-99th percentiles. The least-assumption reading is that they sit near p95 and exactly ONE parent is the whale, which is what production data looks like. targetCounts now builds that shape directly and reconciles the total by nudging the body (the least-constrained part, where a +/-1 is quantile-stable) instead of rescaling a wrong shape until it fits. `max` scales with childN because it is a count of children: the whale keeps its SHARE, so at --scale 0.01 it owns 40% of 200 = 79, not an impossible 7942. VERIFIED live at three scales: real fixture scale 1 -> mean 4.09 / p50 2 / p95 4 / max 7942 over 4894 non-empty of 5000 (exact, including the childless count); scale 0.01 -> p50 2 / max 79; the gate's own fixture -> p50 4 / p95 97 / max 800, STRICTLY BETTER than the old model there too (was p95 72 / max 486). REGRESSION I CAUGHT MID-FIX: I first made the p50->p95 band linear, which averages it at (p50+p95)/2 — for the gate fixture that is 52/parent, so the shape claimed ~32k against 20k and reconcile ate the body down to 1 (declared p50 5 hydrated as p50 1). The band must be GEOMETRIC: these distributions are heavy-tailed, most of that band sits near the median. Covered by TestFanoutHeavyTailIsReproduced at production skew (1942x), which nothing had — the Week-6 gate's fixture is only 40x. Mutation-proven: restoring the old model reports 233 non-empty of 5000 and p50 17, failing it.
tasks.title: `hydrate` — own deterministic synthesis engine
tasks.description: Build the synthesis engine (NOT gofakeit — PRD §16). Given rowshape.yaml + --seed, synthesize N rows into a disposable database. Determinism per RFC §10: per-column PRNG via hash(seed, table, column, row_ordinal) so adding a column or changing --scale does not reshuffle other columns' prefixes; generation order MUST NOT depend on Go map iteration order (canonicalize first). Honor null_fraction within +/-0.5%, honor `unique`, reproduce fan-out DISTRIBUTION shape (p50/p95/max), not just the mean (§6.6); satisfy declared constraints unless orphan_fraction>0 demands otherwise. Substitute obviously-fake values (e.g. user_00417@example.invalid) — realism of shape, not content (RFC §13).
acceptance_criteria: hydrate --seed N is byte-reproducible for the same fixture + hydrator version across platforms.
null_fraction reproduced within +/-0.5%; `unique` honored.
fan-out distribution shape reproduced (p50/p95/max), not just the mean.
Generated values are obviously fake; content realism is explicitly NOT attempted.
Changing --scale does not reshuffle the retained prefix of values.
verification: go test ./internal/hydrate/... (determinism across platforms in CI matrix, null_fraction tolerance, fan-out distribution assertion, scale-prefix stability)

tasks.title: Declared rows vs. hydrated rows separation
tasks.description: Guarantee RFC §9: the fixture always declares production rows (e.g. 1.2M) even when hydrate --scale 0.01 materializes 12k. The data model and hydrate path must keep declared rows (drives later extrapolation) strictly separate from the hydrated count (tests correctness). They MUST NOT be conflated anywhere.
acceptance_criteria: hydrate --scale 0.01 on a fixture declaring 1.2M rows creates ~12k rows while the fixture still reports declared rows = 1.2M.
No code path uses hydrated count where declared rows are meant (guarded by test).
verification: go test ./internal/hydrate/... (scale test asserting declared vs materialized counts diverge correctly)

tasks.audit_note: Criterion 1 ("hydrate spins up a disposable Postgres (testcontainers-go default)") is deliberately superseded by docs/DECISIONS.md D-005, not unimplemented: testcontainers-go pulls the Docker client SDK and dozens of transitive deps, which conflicts with INV-SUPPLY-CHAIN ("single static binary, deps kept deliberately few"), and PRD §17.2 lists the target mechanism as an open question. Ephemeral — a throwaway database on any reachable Postgres, needing only a libpq connection — is the shipped default and gives the same observable behaviour the story asks for. The Target interface keeps testcontainers/pg_tmp/embedded a clean swap-in. Verified by audit, no defect. ONE SMALL DISCREPANCY: D-005 says "Three implementations ship", but Container (the docker-CLI target) is unreachable from the CLI — cmd/ never calls target.NewContainer, and hydrate offers only --target and --ephemeral. It is a swap-in point, not a shipped feature; either expose it behind a flag or reword D-005, which currently reads as though a user can select it.
tasks.title: Disposable hydrate target orchestration
tasks.description: Provide the disposable Postgres target for hydrate: default via testcontainers-go (Docker), and accept a user-provided --target URL. Records open-question 17.2 (testcontainers vs embedded/pg_tmp); ship testcontainers-go as the default now and leave the swap point clean. The container is thrown away after use.
acceptance_criteria: hydrate spins up a disposable Postgres (testcontainers-go default) and tears it down after.
--target <url> hydrates into a provided database instead.
The target abstraction is a single interface so pg_tmp/embedded can be swapped without touching the engine.
verification: go test ./internal/target/... (container lifecycle up/teardown; provided-URL path)

tasks.title: Week-6 gate: reproduce a known pathology end-to-end
tasks.description: Kill-criteria checkpoint (Week 6). Using a small handcrafted pathology fixture (a precursor to the full phase-2 corpus) — e.g. `email` at 3.2% null and a fan-out column with a long tail (max >> mean) — prove pull->rowshape.yaml->hydrate produces a database where a direct query confirms the null ratio and the fan-out tail actually exist. If this fails, STOP and surface: the fixture premise is wrong and `validate` must not be built on it.
acceptance_criteria: A checked-in pathology fixture hydrates into a DB where querying confirms null_fraction is within tolerance of 3.2%.
The fan-out tail is reproduced: measured max fan-out >> mean, matching the fixture's p95/max within tolerance.
This runs in CI as an executable smoke test.
verification: go test ./test/pathology/... (hydrate then SQL-assert null ratio and fan-out tail)

tasks.title: Fan-out + orphan_fraction measurement (the moat field)
tasks.description: Measure the reference fan-out distribution and orphan_fraction (RFC §6.6). The catalog (P1-T3) gives the FK edges and on_delete, but the DISTRIBUTION — children per parent as {mean, p50, p95, max} — and orphan_fraction must be measured by aggregate queries over data, not read from the catalog and not inferred from column stats. In fast mode fan-out is estimated from a sample; orphan_fraction reaches `exact` via a scan (aligns with the phase-1b probes). This is the field no other tool captures and what turns a cascade delete into an outage (RFC §6.6).
acceptance_criteria: Each FK reference carries a fanout distribution {mean, p50, p95, max} with a confidence (estimated in fast mode, measured on a full pass).
orphan_fraction is populated (exact via scan), and nonzero orphans are recorded so a VALIDATE finding can consume them (RFC §6.6, RS-DATA).
Fan-out is measured by aggregation over FK relationships, never inferred from column stats or the catalog alone.
verification: go test against a seeded skewed-fanout DB: p95/max recovered within tolerance; orphan_fraction exact on a DB with known orphans

tasks.title: Skew-aware histograms + partitioned-table shape
tasks.description: Make histograms first-class for skewed columns and capture partitioned-table shape. RFC §6.2: a tenant_id where one tenant owns 80% of rows behaves completely differently under a partitioning migration, and no summary statistic captures that skew — so emit the histogram (standard+ privacy) and reproduce the skew in hydrate, not just the mean/range. RFC §14.2 (leaning): a partitioned table declares a `partitions: {count, strategy, skew}` block with no per-partition entries, so a partitioning migration is reasoned about from the fixture.
spec_refs: RFC §14.2 (partitioned tables: parent declares partitions block)
acceptance_criteria: Skewed columns emit a histogram (bounds/buckets) at privacy standard+; hydrate reproduces the skew, not just the mean/range.
Partitioned tables emit a `partitions: {count, strategy, skew}` block (no per-partition entries).
Histogram bounds honor privacy — dropped under strict (RFC §8.2), since bounds are literally real values.
verification: go test: hydrated distribution reproduces an 80/20 skew within tolerance; a partitioned source emits the partitions block; strict drops histogram bounds

phases.name: Confidence model — HLL, uniqueness probes, auto-escalation, inspect --leaks
phases.ships: The confidence engine: client-side HyperLogLog, exact uniqueness probes, auto-escalation of dangerous columns, and `inspect --leaks`. This is what makes a cheap `fast` fixture safe by construction.
phases.scope_note: This phase upgrades facts from `estimated` toward `measured`/`exact` and adds the self-audit surface. It does not build findings or capping yet (those consume this in phase 2), but the correctness of capping in phase 2 rests entirely on the confidence levels produced here.
definition_of_done: distinct can reach `measured` via client-side HLL with a published error rate.
unique reaches `exact` only via catalog, existence probe, or count comparison — never a sample (INV-UNIQUENESS).
fast mode auto-escalates exactly the dangerous columns (n_distinct/rows > 0.95, no unique constraint), with a cost ceiling that WARNs on what it skips.
`rowshape inspect --leaks` enumerates every value-derived field with its source column and privacy level.

tasks.title: Client-side HyperLogLog distinct estimation
tasks.description: Stream each column with a server-side cursor, hash each value, discard it — bounded memory, no values retained, no server extension (postgresql-hll is unavailable on most managed Postgres, so depending on it would gate the feature behind the infra that most needs it). Sets distinct {confidence: measured, via: hll, error: e}. Precision 14 (~1.6% error, 16KB state) per open-question RFC §14.4 leaning; the error rate is published in distinct.error and feeds §7.4.
acceptance_criteria: distinct via HLL lands at confidence `measured` with `error` populated.
No column values are retained in memory beyond the streaming hash.
Runs without any server-side extension.
Measured error stays within the precision-14 bound on a known-cardinality test column.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/hll/... (accuracy within bound; memory bounded; no-extension path)

tasks.title: Exact uniqueness probes (existence + count)
tasks.description: Implement the two probe-based routes to unique=exact from RFC §7.2: (2) existence probe `SELECT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM t WHERE c IS NOT NULL GROUP BY c HAVING count(*)>1)` — one boolean out, short-circuits on first duplicate; (3) count comparison `SELECT count(*)-count(DISTINCT c) FROM t` — one integer out, gives duplicate count. Route (1), catalog proof, already lands free in P1-T3. If none is affordable, omit `unique` entirely. NEVER infer unique from a sample.
acceptance_criteria: unique reaches `exact` only via constraint, existence probe, or count comparison.
Probes emit no row values (boolean/integer only).
When no route is affordable, `unique` is omitted, not guessed.
A property test confirms unique:true is never produced from sampling.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (probe correctness on unique + non-unique columns; omission path)

tasks.title: Auto-escalation of dangerous columns (default behavior)
tasks.description: In fast mode, automatically escalate to a full pass any column where n_distinct/rows > 0.95 AND no unique constraint exists — exactly the columns that look unique but aren't proven, where a wrong answer costs an outage. Record escalated columns in meta.profile.escalated and set mode `targeted` when any escalation fires. This makes the cheap default safe by construction, not by remembering a flag.
acceptance_criteria: Columns matching the predicate are escalated automatically; others are not.
meta.profile.escalated lists exactly the escalated columns.
mode becomes `targeted` when escalation occurs, `fast` otherwise.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (escalation predicate selects the right column set; escalated list + mode recorded)

tasks.title: Escalation cost ceiling (soft cap + WARN)
tasks.description: Resolve open-question PRD §17.3 / RFC §14.5: a fast pull that quietly full-scans a 400M-row table is a bad surprise. Implement a soft cap (--max-escalation-rows) that, when exceeded, degrades to omitting `unique` for that column and emits a WARN naming exactly what was skipped and why. Silent truncation is forbidden.
acceptance_criteria: Escalation beyond the cap omits `unique` (safe per §7.4) rather than blocking.
A WARN names the skipped column and the row count that triggered the cap.
Default cap is documented; overridable via --max-escalation-rows.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (cap triggers omission + WARN; no silent skip)

tasks.title: `exact` profile mode (full streaming pass)
tasks.description: Implement the `exact` profile mode from RFC §7.3: a full streaming pass per table — HLL for distinct, exact null counts, exact uniqueness probes. Minutes to hours; reads values into emitter memory but emits only aggregates. This is the manual counterpart to auto-escalation for the whole database.
acceptance_criteria: `rowshape pull --exact` performs a full pass; null counts are `exact`, distinct is `measured` via HLL.
Only aggregates are emitted; no row values leave the emitter.
meta.profile.mode records `exact`.
verification: go test ./internal/profile/... (exact-mode confidence levels; aggregate-only output)

tasks.title: `rowshape inspect --leaks`
tasks.description: Enumerate every field in a given fixture derived from row values, with its source column and privacy level (RFC §8.3). A trust feature, not a compliance checkbox: Priya's security team finds these fields anyway; pointing at them first is the difference between a documented tradeoff and an undisclosed leak. Ship in v1 (PRD §11).
acceptance_criteria: Lists every value-derived field (range, histogram bounds, values/frequencies, CHECK expressions, length.max on free_text) with source column + privacy level.
Output is complete: a test fixture with known leaks enumerates all of them and nothing spurious.
Exits non-zero option available for CI gating (documented).
verification: go test ./cmd/... (inspect --leaks on a fixture with seeded value-derived fields lists exactly them)

phases.name: Corpus + capping tests FIRST, then validate + verdict contract + RS-LOCK / RS-DATA
phases.ships: The corpus and capping regression suite, then `validate`, the verdict contract, and the first finding classes (RS-LOCK, RS-DATA, RS-CONSTRAINT, RS-INDEX) with model-based extrapolation across the PG version matrix.
phases.ordering_rule: The corpus (P2-T1) and the capping tests (P2-T2) land BEFORE the findings they test (P2-T8..T11). Reversing this means writing findings, demoing them, then discovering which were wrong on PG 11 (PRD §14, §12).
definition_of_done: rowshape/corpus runs (migration, fixture, expected verdict) triples against every supported PG major (11-17).
Capping tests exist and pass BEFORE the findings, asserting RFC §7.4 (estimated -> WARN, unproven uniqueness cannot PASS).
One Verdict struct with two marshalers renders identically for CLI, MCP, and Action; exit codes 0/1/2/3 honored; verdict is DSSE-signable shaped.

validate hydrates a disposable target, applies the migration through the detected runner, and returns RS-LOCK / RS-DATA / RS-CONSTRAINT / RS-INDEX findings with bucketed, version-conditional estimates and mandatory remediation.

tasks.title: Corpus repo scaffold (rowshape/corpus)
tasks.description: Create rowshape/corpus: executable (migration, fixture, expected verdict) triples covering the documented ways Postgres migrations break — volatile default rewrite, SET NOT NULL full-scan, unique index that can't build, VALIDATE tripping on pre-existing orphans, cascade delete fan-out tail, NOT VALID constraint validated in the same transaction. A runnable harness, not prose. This is an asset people star and cite (PRD §12).
acceptance_criteria: Repo holds at least one triple per pathology named in PRD §12.
Each triple is executable via a test harness that consumes a fixture + migration and asserts the expected verdict.
Harness is parameterized by PG major version (wired to the matrix in P2-T13).
verification: corpus test harness runs green on the seeded triples (against PG 16 initially)

tasks.title: Capping tests FIRST (the wrong-PASS regression suite)
tasks.description: Before any finding exists, write corpus entries that exercise RFC §7.4 capping: a fixture profiling a column at `estimated` cannot yield PASS for a finding depending on it (must WARN naming the resolving command); uniqueness that is unproven cannot certify ADD CONSTRAINT UNIQUE. These are untested assertions until something exercises them — and a wrong PASS is unrecoverable (PRD §11, §15). Write these before the findings they cap.
acceptance_criteria: A triple where the relevant fact is `estimated` asserts a WARN (not PASS) with the resolving command named.
A triple with unproven uniqueness asserts ADD CONSTRAINT UNIQUE cannot PASS.
These tests are committed and RED (pending the capping engine P2-T4) — they define the contract findings must satisfy.
verification: corpus capping suite present; goes GREEN only once P2-T4 lands

tasks.found_by_audit: `location` was never populated on any finding — locationFor was a one-line stub, `func locationFor(_ validate.Statement) *verdict.Location { return nil }`, with a discarded parameter. Found by comparing the REAL --json output to PRD §10's documented example field-for-field, which this story's criterion 1 requires: top-level matched exactly, but the finding was missing `location`, which the example shows as {file, line}. Not an oversight nobody would notice: verdict.Location{File,Line} exists, the human renderer already prints "at file:line" when set, and P4-T2's entire acceptance is "Each finding with a `location` produces a PR annotation at the correct file and line" — that story would have been built on a field that is always nil. ROOT CAUSE: validate.Statement carried no origin, and SplitStatements(sql) []string discarded position before Apply ever saw it. FIXED: validate.Located{SQL,File,Line} + SplitStatementsIn(file, sql) track the 1-based line via a newline prefix-count (O(1) per lookup) with a trim-aware offset, so blank lines between statements do not shift it; Statement carries File/Line; Apply threads them; locationFor returns them. SplitStatements keeps its signature as a wrapper for callers that only want text. Line points at the start of the statement AS WRITTEN, counting a leading comment as part of it — where a reviewer looks, and it needs no second parse. ALSO NORMALIZED: paths go through filepath.ToSlash, because the verdict is the public contract and is DSSE-signable (INV-DSSE-SHAPE) — a backslash path on Windows vs a forward-slash one on Linux would make the same migration produce two different documents, and annotations want repo-style paths. Verified live: a two-statement file reports lines 1 and 5 and the human output shows "at migML/001.sql:1". Inline SQL (an agent handing over what it just wrote) has no file and gets nil rather than an invented path. TEST NOTE: the first mutation check PASSED — stubbing locationFor back to nil broke no findings test, because none asserted location. TestFindingCarriesLocation now fails on it. The estimate block also carries a `confidence` field beyond the §10 example, which is correct per INV-DURATIONS-BUCKETS (the extrapolation basis is attached); the example is illustrative, not exhaustive.
tasks.title: Verdict struct + two marshalers (DSSE-signable shape)
tasks.description: Implement the one `Verdict` struct in internal/verdict matching PRD §10 exactly: rowshape, verdict, fixture{id,digest}, duration_ms, findings[] with code/severity/title/location/detail/evidence/estimate/depends_on/confidence/remediation/explain. Two marshalers: JSON and a human renderer that is a RENDERING of the struct, never a separate code path. Exit codes 0 PASS / 1 FAIL / 2 WARN-only / 3 tool error. Shape it as a DSSE/in-toto predicate from day one (§9.1): subject = fixture digest + migration file digests; predicateType = https://rowshape.com/attestation/v1.
acceptance_criteria: JSON output matches the PRD §10 example schema (field-for-field).
Human output is produced by rendering the same struct (proven by a test that both marshalers consume one Verdict value).
Exit codes map 0/1/2/3 as specified; WARN-only is configurable to fail.
Verdict serializes as a DSSE predicate with subject = fixture digest + migration file digests; predicateType is the owned URI.
remediation is required (non-empty) on every finding with severity error.
verification: go test ./internal/verdict/... (JSON golden vs PRD §10; single-struct/two-marshaler test; exit-code table; DSSE predicate shape; remediation-required)

tasks.known_defect: RESOLVED (was OPEN). Found by driving `validate` against a fixture from a REAL pull. A finding's table name came from the migration SQL as written, but RFC §5 keys fixture tables by QUALIFIED name, so `ALTER TABLE users` (ordinary SQL — Postgres resolves it via search_path) missed the key `public.users`. FIRST REPORT UNDERSTATED IT: the miss is not only an unresolvable depends_on path (which fails safe: absent ranks below every level, so capping caps to WARN and no wrong PASS is possible). The analyzers ALSO look up f.Tables[table] with that raw name, so a miss silently yields the ZERO VALUE. Proven live, same statement, same 50M-row table, only qualification differing: `ALTER TABLE public.orders ADD COLUMN ... DEFAULT clock_timestamp()` -> estimate `outage`; `ALTER TABLE orders ...` -> estimate `instant`. Both WARN, so only the bucket gave it away — rowshape told the user an outage was instant, which is its core promise inverted. FIXED: fixture.ResolveTable maps a SQL table name onto the fixture's own key — exact match wins; otherwise an unqualified name matches on the part after the last dot and ONLY when exactly one table matches. It never assumes a default schema. Ambiguity (public.users AND tenant.users) stays unresolved, which preserves the old safe behavior (no facts -> capped to WARN) rather than answering from the wrong table. The single-match case is also what actually happens during validate: the migration is applied to a database hydrated FROM THIS FIXTURE, so its tables are the only ones that exist. Analyzers route every SQL-derived table name through it; the SQL parsers stay pure and resolution happens at the callers. DSSE concern from the first report is moot and inverted: depends_on now carries the canonical fixture key, which is strictly more accurate for an attestation. TEST NOTE: the first end-to-end test was VACUOUS and passed against the bug — a bare Capture has no hydrated row count or measured duration, so estimateFor extrapolates from 1ms at declared==hydrated and every bucket is `instant`, including the control. The test now supplies both and guards the guard (it fails if the qualified control is not itself alarming). Mutation-tested: removing the resolution reports outage -> instant. Verified live: both forms now report `outage` with confidence `estimated`. FOLLOW-UP, NOW ALSO FIXED: when a table genuinely cannot be resolved (typo, never pulled, or ambiguous across schemas), the estimator read rows=0 and reported `instant` — arithmetic cannot tell 'no rows' from 'never seen this table', and answers instant for both. estimateFor now refuses to extrapolate unless the fixture actually carries the table, mirroring RFC §9.1's refusal without engine.version: the finding is still reported (the lock is real, read off the SQL) but with no bucket, a title saying 'duration not extrapolated: <table> is not in the fixture', and a detail naming the fix (re-run `rowshape pull`, or qualify the name). Mutation-tested: disabling the refusal reports `instant` for an unknown table. Also fixed the display it exposed — an absent confidence rendered as `[confidence: ]`, blank on the one line whose job is to say how well the answer is known; it now reads `[confidence: absent]`, the word RFC §7.4 uses. JSON is untouched (null already means absent; the contract is the public API, INV-VERDICT-STABLE).
tasks.title: Confidence capping engine
tasks.description: Each finding declares `depends_on` (fixture facts). The verdict a finding can produce is capped by the MINIMUM confidence across its dependencies: exact/measured -> PASS allowed; estimated/declared/absent -> WARN max. A capped finding produces a WARN naming the command that resolves it. Findings MUST NOT downgrade a dependency's confidence to reach a stronger verdict — enforce this in code, not by convention. Turns P2-T2 GREEN.
acceptance_criteria: Verdict severity is capped at the min confidence of the finding's declared dependencies.
estimated/absent dependency -> WARN with a resolving command string.
No API allows lowering a dependency's confidence to raise a verdict (structurally prevented).
The P2-T2 capping corpus suite passes.
verification: go test ./internal/verdict/... + corpus capping suite green

tasks.title: Model-based, version-conditional extrapolation
tasks.description: Extrapolate cost/duration per operation class along a known model (table rewrite O(n), constraint validation O(n), b-tree build O(n log n), CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY O(n log n) two-pass no exclusive lock, catalog-only O(1)) using DECLARED rows, not hydrated count. Cost models are engine-version-conditional (PG11+ catalog fast-path for non-volatile defaults; volatile default still rewrites; SET NOT NULL still full-scans). MUST refuse to extrapolate when engine.version is absent. Report buckets (instant/fast/noticeable/slow/outage) with basis attached; estimate confidence never exceeds the confidence of `rows`.
acceptance_criteria: Each operation class maps to its model; extrapolation uses declared rows.
Refuses to extrapolate (and says so) when engine.version is missing.
Findings report buckets with {model, basis_rows, basis_ms, declared_rows}; never a point estimate.
A version-conditional case differs correctly between PG 11 and PG 16 (asserted in corpus).
verification: go test ./internal/estimate/... (model table; version-conditional divergence; no-version refusal; bucket output)

tasks.title: Runner detection (Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle, raw SQL)
tasks.description: Detect the user's migration runner and shell out to it — orchestrate, do not reimplement. Support Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle, and plain raw-SQL directories (psql -f). Rowshape applies the migration set through the detected runner against the disposable target only.
acceptance_criteria: Auto-detects Alembic / Prisma / Drizzle / raw-SQL dir from the repo.
Shells out to the detected tool; no migration logic is reimplemented.
Detection is overridable/explicitly selectable.
verification: go test ./internal/runner/... (detection fixtures per tool; shell-out invocation)

tasks.title: `validate` orchestration (the main event)
tasks.description: Wire `rowshape validate [--json]`: hydrate a disposable Postgres (from P1-T9), apply the current migration set via the detected runner (P2-T6), and capture success/failure, per-statement wall time, lock mode + duration, rows affected, constraint violations, and index build behavior. Feed captures into findings + extrapolation + capping and emit the Verdict. HARD-REFUSE if the target host matches the fixture source host; never touch a non-disposable DB.
acceptance_criteria: validate hydrates, applies, captures the six signal classes, and returns a Verdict.
--json emits the machine verdict; default emits the human rendering of the same struct.
Refuses when target host == fixture source host; only operates on disposable/provided targets.
validate makes no network call to any cloud service.
When run against a provided live branch/target (e.g. --target $NEON_BRANCH_URL), the relevant facts are upgraded to `exact` (PRD §15, branching complementarity).
verification: go test ./cmd/... (end-to-end validate on a corpus triple); assert host-match refusal; assert no cloud egress

tasks.title: RS-LOCK findings (lock mode + rewrite duration)
tasks.description: Detect lock-mode and table-rewrite pathologies: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE locks, volatile-default rewrites, type changes. Report lock_mode, rows_rewritten, bucketed duration via the extrapolation engine, and mandatory expand/backfill/contract remediation. Version-conditional (consume engine.version). Corpus entry precedes/accompanies this per ordering rule.
acceptance_criteria: RS-LOCK-001 fires on the volatile-default rewrite corpus case with correct lock_mode + rows_rewritten evidence.
Duration is a bucket with basis; remediation is the split expand/backfill/contract recipe.
Finding declares depends_on (e.g. public.<t>.rows) and carries capped confidence.
Behaves version-conditionally between PG 11 and PG 16.
verification: corpus RS-LOCK cases green across PG matrix

tasks.title: RS-DATA findings (uniqueness, null-on-NOT-NULL, orphans)
tasks.description: Detect data-shape pathologies: ADD CONSTRAINT UNIQUE on a column whose uniqueness is unproven (must WARN via capping, e.g. RS-DATA-014), SET NOT NULL / NOT NULL column where null_fraction > 0, FOREIGN KEY ... VALIDATE against nonzero orphan_fraction. Every finding declares depends_on and is capped.
acceptance_criteria: RS-DATA-014 warns (never PASS) when uniqueness is `estimated`/absent, naming `rowshape pull --exact <col>`.
A NOT NULL addition against null_fraction>0 is flagged.
A VALIDATE against orphan_fraction>0 is flagged as will-fail.
All RS-DATA findings carry depends_on + capped confidence.
verification: corpus RS-DATA cases green; capping suite (P2-T2) satisfied by RS-DATA-014

tasks.title: RS-CONSTRAINT findings
tasks.description: Detect constraint pathologies: adding/validating CHECK or FK constraints that conflict with existing data, NOT VALID constraint validated in the same transaction, constraint validation full-scan cost. Consume constraints (§6.4) and validated flags. Bucketed, capped, remediated.
acceptance_criteria: Flags NOT VALID + VALIDATE in the same transaction.
Flags constraint additions that conflict with profiled data shape.
Reports validation scan cost as a bucket; remediation mandatory.
verification: corpus RS-CONSTRAINT cases green across matrix

tasks.title: RS-INDEX findings
tasks.description: Detect index pathologies: CREATE INDEX (non-concurrent) taking an exclusive lock, unique index that can't build on non-unique data, rebuild time driven by index bytes/bloat_estimate (§6.5). Recommend CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY where applicable. Bucketed via O(n log n) model, capped, remediated.
spec_refs: RFC §9.1 (b-tree O(n log n), CIC two-pass)
acceptance_criteria: Flags non-concurrent index builds that lock; recommends CONCURRENTLY.
Flags a unique index that cannot build given profiled uniqueness.
Rebuild duration bucket uses index bytes/bloat as basis.
verification: corpus RS-INDEX cases green across matrix

tasks.title: `rowshape explain <CODE>` (agent-readable remediation)
tasks.description: Implement `explain <CODE>` returning docs + remediation for a finding code, agent-readable, no web search required. Every error finding's `explain` field points here. remediation is mandatory (a finding an agent can't act on is a bug).
acceptance_criteria: explain RS-LOCK-001 (and every shipped code) returns structured remediation.
Content is data-driven from the same source the finding cites (no drift).
Every shipped finding code has an explain entry (test-enforced).
verification: go test ./cmd/... (explain covers every registered finding code)

tasks.title: PG version matrix in corpus CI (11-17)
tasks.description: Run the corpus (including capping and all finding classes) against every supported Postgres major, 11 through 17. A finding right on 16 can be wrong on 11; the matrix is what catches it (PRD §12). Extends the P0-T6 matrix-ready CI.
acceptance_criteria: CI runs the corpus against PG 11,12,13,14,15,16,17.
Version-conditional cases assert different verdicts per major where expected.
A regression on any major fails CI.
verification: CI matrix green across all seven majors
tasks.note: SECOND CORRECTION (later audit): the matrix ran `go test ./corpus/...` alone, and the corpus never calls `pull` — so every other package that speaks SQL to a real server (internal/profile's catalog reader, internal/target, test/pathology) ran on PG 16 ONLY, via ci.yml's build-test job. The matrix existed but was not pointed at the code most likely to carry version bugs. Widening it to `go test ./corpus/... ./internal/profile/... ./internal/target/... ./test/pathology/...` found, within minutes, that `rowshape pull` COULD NOT READ A CATALOG AT ALL on PG 10/11: pg_attribute.attgenerated arrived in PG12 and the columns query selected it unguarded (ERROR: column a.attgenerated does not exist, SQLSTATE 42703). Not a degraded fixture — no fixture. rowshape claims PG 11-17, so the product did not work on a supported version, and no user could have learned that except by pointing it at their database. Damning detail: an identical guard already sat ten lines below for pg_index.indnullsnotdistinct (PG15+) — one version-conditional column was guarded, its neighbour was not, and nothing could tell the difference. Fixed with the same degrade-to-constant shape. A second PG13 assumption surfaced in the same run: the profile test seed used gen_random_uuid() (core only on PG13+; the corpus harness had already worked around it with CREATE EXTENSION pgcrypto, but this seed had never run below 13) — now md5(g::text)::uuid, extension-free and deterministic. VERIFIED: all eight majors 10-17 green with the widened matrix. ORIGINAL CORRECTION: this story was marked passes:true while the corpus matrix was RED on PG 10, 11 and 12 on every run since it landed — acceptance criterion 1 ('CI runs the corpus against PG 11,12,13,14,15,16,17') was false for two of the seven supported majors. Found by checking actual CI history (`gh run list`) rather than trusting local `go test ./...`, which passes because every Postgres-backed test skips silently unless ROWSHAPE_TEST_PG_DSN is set — the same vacuous-green pattern that hid the P3-T10 hook tests and the RedactURL leak. ROOT CAUSE: `DROP DATABASE ... WITH (FORCE)` is PG13+; on 11/12 it is a syntax error, so 'reset disposable database: ERROR: syntax error at or near "WITH"' killed every triple before it validated anything. This was a PRODUCT bug, not only a test one — internal/target/target.go used the clause on the --ephemeral path, so `rowshape validate --ephemeral` against a PG 11/12 server failed outright; the comment there already said '(PG13+)' but the guard was never written. Fixed with target.dropDatabase, which reads server_version_num (integer form, e.g. 110022/160004) and uses WITH (FORCE) on 13+ or terminate-backends-then-drop on 11/12 (what FORCE does internally). The two test helpers (cmd/plan_verify_test.go, cmd/mcp/tool_plan_against_test.go) carried the identical assumption and were fixed the same way. This is precisely the failure PRD §14/§12 named as the reason to build the matrix BEFORE the findings — 'writing findings, demoing them, then discovering which were wrong on PG 11'. PG 11 and 12 had never been exercised. PG 10 stays in the matrix deliberately (D-006/D-007: the catalog fast-path boundary lands in 11, so 10 is what makes the version-conditional cases diverge). VERIFIED on real CI (PR #3): the corpus matrix is green on all eight majors 10-17 for the first time — PG 11 and 12 had never passed. The first attempt at the fix REGRESSED every major: `SHOW server_version_num` returns TEXT (OID 25), so scanning it into *int failed before the version test and killed 13-17 too, turning a 3-major failure into an 8-major one. `SELECT current_setting('server_version_num')::int` returns a real integer. CI caught that within one cycle; local `go test ./...` could not, because it skips every DB-backed test. Note for future loops: this environment has no working Docker daemon, but it DOES have a PostgreSQL 18 service listening on localhost:5432 (initially and wrongly assumed absent) — authenticating to it would let the DB suite run locally via ROWSHAPE_TEST_PG_DSN and catch this class of bug before CI. PG 18 also exercises the 13+ WITH (FORCE) path specifically.

tasks.title: `validate --calibrate` (upgrade estimate to measured)
tasks.description: Implement --calibrate: hydrate at two scale factors, fit the actual cost curve, and upgrade the estimate confidence from `estimated` to `measured` for the migration you're genuinely nervous about. Slower and honest (RFC §9.2).
acceptance_criteria: --calibrate hydrates at two scales and fits the curve.
Calibrated estimate is marked confidence `measured` with basis from the two points.
Without --calibrate, estimates remain `estimated` (no silent upgrade).
verification: go test ./internal/estimate/... (two-point fit; confidence upgrade only under --calibrate)

tasks.found_by_audit: verify was one-directional and said reality matched intent when it did not. Found by driving `verify --against` at a live database and then adding a column the fixture does not declare: it reported '[OK] reality matches intent', exit 0. compareSchema only walked the FIXTURE, answering 'is everything I declared still there?', so anything present on the target but absent from the fixture was invisible. That is the commonest drift (a migration shipped without a re-pull) and the most consequential: validate hydrates FROM the fixture, so every later verdict reasons about a schema production no longer has — confidently and wrongly — and verify is the one command meant to catch it. PRD §8.1 states the contract as 'does reality match intent', which runs in both directions. FIXED: compareSchema now also reports columns present on the target and absent from the fixture, with the remediation (re-run `rowshape pull`). Deliberately scoped to tables the fixture DOES declare: a fixture may legitimately cover a subset (`pull --schema public`), so an undeclared TABLE is not evidence of anything and flagging it would make verify noisy enough to ignore — which costs more than the drift it catches. An undeclared COLUMN in a table the fixture claims to describe is unambiguous. Verified live: clean target still exits 0, the undeclared column now exits 1 naming the column. Mutation-proven, plus negative tests (subset tables are not drift; identical schemas report nothing). The story's own acceptance criteria (read-only enforced, dry-run applies nothing) were and remain met — this was an unstated gap, not a false passes:true, and read-only was re-verified live by fingerprinting the schema before and after.
tasks.title: `plan --against` and `verify --against` (read-only)
tasks.description: Implement `plan --against prod` (dry-run diff vs a live target) and `verify --against prod` (post-deploy: does reality match intent). verify is read-only by definition. Validating against a live branch/target should upgrade the relevant facts to `exact` (PRD §15, Neon complementarity). These are the non-mutating live-target surfaces from §8.1.
acceptance_criteria: plan --against produces a dry-run diff without applying anything.
verify --against performs read-only checks only (no writes, enforced).
Validating against a provided live target upgrades the relevant facts to `exact`.
verification: go test ./cmd/... (plan diff; verify read-only enforcement; fact-upgrade on live target)

tasks.title: Fixture-spec conformance suite + published JSON Schema
tasks.description: The spec's strategic value is that others can emit it (PRD §3, §16). Ship, in rowshape/fixture-spec: (1) a published machine-readable JSON Schema for rowshape.yaml (format version "1"), and (2) a conformance test suite encoding the three RFC §13 profiles — a conformant emitter (read-only, strict on demand, never `range` on text, never `unique` from a sample, confidence on every fact, stable digest), a conformant hydrator (null_fraction ±0.5%, honor `unique`, reproduce fanout shape, deterministic), and a conformant validator (cap per §7.4, buckets per §9.2, never downgrade confidence). rowshape's own CLI must pass it and a third party can run it against their emitter — this is what makes the spec a position rather than an aspiration.
acceptance_criteria: A JSON Schema for rowshape.yaml (format version "1") is published in the fixture-spec repo and validates the reference fixtures.
A conformance suite encodes the RFC §13 emitter/hydrator/validator MUSTs and is runnable by third parties.
rowshape's own pull/hydrate/validate pass the suite in CI, and the suite REJECTS a deliberately non-conformant fixture (range on text, unique-from-sample).
verification: conformance CI green against rowshape; suite rejects seeded non-conformant fixtures; JSON Schema validates all reference fixtures

tasks.title: Tool-error (exit 3) taxonomy — broken tool vs unsafe migration
tasks.description: An agent must never confuse 'the tool couldn't run' with 'the migration is unsafe.' Define and implement the exit-code-3 tool-error path for the operational failures that are neither PASS/FAIL/WARN: no Docker daemon / disposable target unavailable (PRD §17.2), can't connect to the target, runner not found or failed to invoke, fixture parse error, and an unknown fixture major version — which MUST refuse rather than best-effort (RFC §12). Each returns exit 3 with a structured, agent-readable reason clearly distinct from a verdict.
acceptance_criteria: All listed operational failures return exit code 3 with a structured reason, never a FAIL verdict.
An unknown fixture major version refuses with exit 3 (RFC §12) — never a partial-understanding PASS/FAIL.
The tool-error payload is machine-readable (an agent can branch on it) and its human rendering comes from the same shape.
verification: go test: each failure mode yields exit 3 + structured reason; unknown-major fixture refused; FAIL vs tool-error never conflated

phases.name: MCP subcommand + init --agent + the agent rule
