phases.ships: `rowshape mcp` (the wedge): a subcommand of the same binary exposing four brutally-thin tools on the official Go SDK, plus `rowshape init` / `init --agent` which writes MCP config, the versioned agent rule, and a pre-commit backstop so an agent actually reaches for rowshape unprompted.
phases.scope_note: This phase closes the loop that the whole bet rests on (PRD §2, §15): getting rowshape into the agent's context and turn. The MCP tools are thin wrappers over surfaces already built in phase 2 (validate, explain, plan) and phase 1 (the fixture) — no new finding logic here. The differentiator is discipline: compact codes over fat schemas (MCPFold), and treating the agent rule as a versioned product artifact, not a README snippet.
definition_of_done: `rowshape init` detects the stack (engine + migration runner: Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle, raw-SQL dir) and scaffolds a committable config without touching a production database.
`rowshape mcp` serves the four tools (describe_shape, validate_migration, explain_finding, plan_against) over the official Go SDK as a subcommand of the single binary — not a separate artifact.
MCP schemas are thin: findings return compact codes with explain_finding as the only expansion path, and no tool returns a full fixture unless a specific table is requested (PRD §8.2).

`rowshape init --agent` writes MCP client config, a versioned agent rule into AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / .cursor/rules, and a pre-commit hook running `rowshape validate` — all idempotent and re-runnable.
The agent rule ships versioned in the repo so improvements reach existing users on upgrade (PRD §8.3).

tasks.title: `rowshape init` — detect stack, scaffold config
tasks.description: Implement the base `rowshape init` from PRD §8.1: detect the database engine and the migration runner in use (Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle, or a plain SQL directory) and scaffold a committable rowshape config. Detection only — init never connects to a production database and never runs a migration. This is the foundation `init --agent` (P3-T8..T10) extends.
spec_refs: PRD §8 (runner detection: Alembic, Prisma, Drizzle, raw SQL)
acceptance_criteria: `rowshape init` detects the migration runner from repo layout (alembic.ini, prisma/schema.prisma, drizzle config, or a SQL migrations directory).
It writes a committable config file recording engine + detected runner; no network or database access occurs.
Re-running init is idempotent — it does not clobber user edits to the config.
verification: go test ./cmd/... (runner detection across Alembic/Prisma/Drizzle/raw-SQL fixtures; idempotent re-run; no DB connection attempted)

tasks.title: `rowshape mcp` subcommand scaffold on the official Go SDK
tasks.description: Wire `rowshape mcp` as a subcommand of the single binary (not a separate artifact) using modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk over stdio. Register the four tool handlers (filled by P3-T3..T6) and advertise `rowshape_fixture` major-version compatibility per RFC §12. This is the wedge surface from PRD §8.2; the SDK is past v1.0.0 with a no-breaking-changes guarantee (PRD §7).
spec_refs: PRD §7 (official Go MCP SDK, past v1.0.0)
acceptance_criteria: `rowshape mcp` starts a stdio MCP server built on modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk within the same binary.
The server registers exactly the four tools named in PRD §8.2 and no others.
Server initialization completes a handshake with a reference MCP client in a test.
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (server boots, lists exactly four tools, completes initialize handshake against an in-process client)

tasks.title: MCP tool: describe_shape
tasks.description: Expose `describe_shape` — hand the agent the shape before it writes SQL (PRD §8.2). Reads a committed fixture and returns a compact view of a requested table's relevant facts (row count, null fractions, distinct/uniqueness confidence, fan-out, format classes). Never returns a full fixture unless a specific table is requested; the whole point is that the agent reasons over four kilobytes, not forty gigabytes (RFC §2).
spec_refs: PRD §8.2 (describe_shape; never return a full fixture unless asked for a table)
acceptance_criteria: describe_shape returns the shape for a named table, including confidence on each fact (RFC §7).
With no table specified it returns a table index/summary, not the full fixture body.
Output is compact — value-derived facts are summarized, honoring the fixture's privacy level (RFC §8).
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (describe_shape returns per-table shape + confidence; refuses to dump full fixture without a table target)

tasks.title: MCP tool: validate_migration (the loop-closer)
tasks.description: Expose `validate_migration` — the loop-closer from PRD §8.2. Wraps the phase-2 `validate` orchestration and returns the Verdict as compact findings codes (RS-LOCK-001, etc.) plus the overall verdict and exit-code-equivalent, not prose. This is the tool that lets an agent write → validate → read the failure → fix → re-validate inside its own turn (PRD §2).
acceptance_criteria: validate_migration invokes the same validate path as the CLI and returns the same Verdict struct (one struct, PRD §10).
Findings are returned as compact codes + minimal fields, with `explain_finding` as the expansion path (not full remediation prose inline).
The tool surfaces the PASS/FAIL/WARN verdict and honors confidence capping (RFC §7.4) — an estimated-dependency finding never reports PASS.
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (validate_migration returns the shared Verdict; compact codes; capping preserved end-to-end)

tasks.title: MCP tool: explain_finding
tasks.description: Expose `explain_finding` — remediation without a web search (PRD §8.2). Wraps `rowshape explain <CODE>` (P2-T12) so an agent that received a compact code from validate_migration can expand exactly the finding it hit. This is the deliberate expansion path that keeps validate_migration's schema thin (MCPFold discipline).
acceptance_criteria: explain_finding returns the same remediation content as the `rowshape explain <CODE>` CLI path.
Every error-severity finding code resolves to actionable remediation (a finding an agent can't act on is a bug, PRD §10).
An unknown code returns a clean tool error, not a crash.
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (explain_finding matches CLI explain output; unknown code handled gracefully)

tasks.title: MCP tool: plan_against
tasks.description: Expose `plan_against` — what would change on target X (PRD §8.2). Wraps the phase-2 `plan --against` dry-run (P2-T15). Read-only: returns the diff a migration would produce against a live target without applying anything, and (per PRD §15) validating against a live target upgrades the relevant facts to `exact`.
acceptance_criteria: plan_against returns a dry-run diff against a provided target and applies nothing.
It reuses the CLI `plan --against` path (no reimplementation).
Read-only is enforced — the tool never issues a write against the target.
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (plan_against returns dry-run diff; write-refusal enforced)

tasks.title: Thin-schema / MCPFold discipline + cross-link
tasks.description: Enforce the token-discipline PRD §8.2 demands: four fat tool schemas in every session costs tokens and earns resentment. Keep schemas brutally thin, return findings as compact codes with explain_finding as the expansion, and never return a full fixture unless a specific table is asked for. Add a schema-size guard test and cross-link MCPFold in both READMEs (rowshape as MCPFold's flagship consumer).
spec_refs: PRD §8.2 (MCPFold-shaped discipline; thin schemas; compact codes)
acceptance_criteria: A guard test asserts each tool's advertised input/output schema stays under a fixed token/character budget.
No tool returns a full fixture body by default; describe_shape requires a table target for detail.
README (CLI repo) and MCPFold README cross-link, naming rowshape as a flagship MCPFold consumer.
verification: go test ./cmd/mcp/... (schema-budget guard passes for all four tools)
manual: both READMEs cross-link
tasks.note: Budgets set from measurement, not guesswork: per-tool 700 chars (~175 tokens) against an actual 310-510, and 2400 chars (~2400/4 = ~600 tokens) for the whole four-tool session tax against an actual 1734 (~433). Characters are the unit — a stable proxy needing no tokenizer, ~chars/4 for this mixed English+JSON. The guard was proven to BITE: fattening describe_shape's `table` description with one realistic over-helpful sentence pushed it to 779 and failed the test with a message that names the fix (cut a parameter / shorten / move behind explain_finding) rather than inviting a budget raise. Two payload guards accompany it: describe_shape's default answer is size-capped at 4096 chars (index-only shape was already asserted in tool_describe_shape_test.go; this guards the SIZE, which erodes silently), and validate_migration findings are asserted to carry a named explain path and to inline none of remediation/detail/evidence/explanation. Cross-links landed both ways: rowshape README gained a 'For agents' section + MCPFold under Related repos, and MCPFold's README gained a 'The other half: servers that don't overspend' subsection under Why mcpfold naming rowshape a flagship consumer (that repo is separate — dj-pearson/MCPFold — and its edit is committed there independently). docs/mcp.md documents all four tools, the capping guarantee on the MCP surface, and the budget table. Verified: go test ./cmd/mcp/ green, full go test ./... green, gofmt clean, go vet clean, all relative doc links resolve.

tasks.title: `init --agent` — write MCP client config
tasks.description: First of the three things `init --agent` writes (PRD §8.3): MCP config for the detected client — `.mcp.json`, or the client's own config path (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.). Detect the client from the repo/environment and register `rowshape mcp` as a server. An MCP server the agent never calls is decoration (PRD §8.3); this is what actually wires it in.
spec_refs: PRD §8.3 (init --agent item 1: MCP config for the detected client)
acceptance_criteria: `rowshape init --agent` writes a valid MCP config registering `rowshape mcp` for the detected client.
The correct path/format is chosen per client (.mcp.json or the client's own location).
Writing is idempotent — re-running merges rather than duplicating the server entry.
verification: go test ./cmd/... (MCP config written for each supported client; idempotent merge; valid JSON)
tasks.note: Client table (supportedMCPClients) drives path + format per client, because the formats have diverged and a file in the wrong shape is silently ignored — indistinguishable from rowshape not working: Claude Code .mcp.json/mcpServers, Cursor .cursor/mcp.json/mcpServers, VS Code .vscode/mcp.json/`servers` + explicit "type": "stdio". Detection returns EVERY client present (a Cursor user and a Claude Code user in one repo is ordinary), from repo markers plus the CLAUDECODE env var so a marker-less repo is still detected when the agent itself runs init; falls back to .mcp.json (de-facto standard, committable) when nothing is found. Only repo-local committable paths are written — machine-global config is a different consent surface and isn't committable. The registered command is the bare name `rowshape` from PATH, never the absolute path of the binary running init, because these files get committed and an absolute path breaks the rest of the team. Merge preserves all other servers and unrelated top-level keys; an already-correct entry is a true no-op (file untouched) via a JSON-normalized comparison — without normalize() the round-trip type skew (float64/[]any) would make it rewrite every run. Unparseable (e.g. JSONC) config is REFUSED with exit 3 + the entry to paste, never clobbered. Verified beyond green tests: drove the real binary on a demo repo with a pre-existing github server + .vscode — correct merge, both clients wired, correct per-client shapes, byte-identical no-op re-run. Mutation-tested the three load-bearing behaviors (clobber-instead-of-merge, never-detect-unchanged, wrong VS Code root key); each is caught by a distinct test (the first attempt at the idempotency mutation was a build error, so it was redone to confirm the guard genuinely bites). KNOWN TRADEOFF, documented in docs/mcp.md: the first merge into an existing config reformats it (JSON round-trip re-indents and sorts keys). Data-preserving and one-time (output is stable, later runs are byte-identical), but the first --agent diff touches lines the user didn't add. Order-preserving edits need a JSON AST dep, which PRD §7 (deps deliberately few) doesn't justify for this. Zed deliberately unsupported: `context_servers` with a different entry shape in a JSONC settings file whose format has moved across versions — a half-right entry there is worse than an honest omission.

tasks.title: `init --agent` — the versioned agent rule
tasks.description: Second thing `init --agent` writes (PRD §8.3): an agent rule into AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / .cursor/rules — before writing a migration call describe_shape; before opening a PR call validate_migration; never hand-wave a FAIL. The rule text is a product artifact iterated against real agent sessions like a prompt, and it ships versioned in the repo so improvements reach existing users on upgrade.
spec_refs: PRD §8.3 (init --agent item 2: agent rule; versioned; reaches existing users on upgrade)
acceptance_criteria: `init --agent` writes the rule into the detected agent-rule file(s) (AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / .cursor/rules).
The rule text is stored versioned in the repo and carries a version marker so `init` can update it in place on upgrade.
Re-running updates an outdated managed block without clobbering surrounding user content.
verification: go test ./cmd/... (rule written to each target file; versioned managed-block update replaces old rule, preserves user text)
tasks.note: Rule text lives in internal/agentrule/rule.md, go:embed'd so the binary stays self-contained (PRD §7), with agentrule.Version (plain int, not semver — there is no backward-incompatible prompt, and the only question a consumer asks is 'is this older than what I ship'). Managed block delimited by HTML comments so it renders invisibly in Markdown — the agent reads the instruction, not the plumbing. The versioning requirement is load-bearing and drove the design: blockRE matches a begin marker at ANY version (and a malformed one, treated as older-than-anything and repaired), so an upgrade REPLACES in place rather than appending a second rule that contradicts the first. Content outside the block returns byte-for-byte: AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md are the user's files and rowshape is a guest in them. Targets follow the conventions the repo already keeps (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, .cursor/rules/rowshape.mdc), falling back to AGENTS.md — the cross-tool convention, not tied to one vendor — when it keeps none; it writes to what's in use rather than littering. Cursor's .mdc gets `alwaysApply: true` frontmatter, seeded only on create so user edits to it survive (an .mdc without it is a rule Cursor loads only when it feels like it — indistinguishable from not writing one). Rule content verified accurate against what is actually built: real finding codes (RS-LOCK-001, RS-DATA-014), real verdict semantics (WARN = rests on an unproven fact, names the resolving command), and it tells the agent to ASK rather than run `pull` itself, since creating a fixture means reading a real database (a human's call). Budget guard at 2000 chars (~500 tokens); actual 1697 (~424). Budget deliberately set from a principled ceiling, NOT snug to current text — the first draft came in at 1696/1800 (6% headroom), which would fire on every honest iteration of text that is explicitly meant to be iterated, and teach everyone to raise the budget instead of respecting it. Verified beyond green tests: drove the real binary through the upgrade path (a v0 block with user content above AND below → v1 in place, both preserved, exactly one block). Mutation-tested the three load-bearing behaviors — append-instead-of-replace, regex matching only the current version (stale block never found), and clobbering the whole file instead of the block span — each caught by a distinct test.

tasks.title: `init --agent` — pre-commit hook backstop
tasks.description: Third thing `init --agent` writes (PRD §8.3): a pre-commit hook running `rowshape validate` — the backstop for when the agent ignores the rule, which it sometimes will. Install a hook (or extend an existing pre-commit config) that blocks a commit on a FAIL verdict, so the guarantee doesn't depend solely on the agent honoring the rule.
spec_refs: PRD §8.3 (init --agent item 3: pre-commit hook running rowshape validate)
acceptance_criteria: `init --agent` installs a pre-commit hook that runs `rowshape validate` and blocks the commit on a FAIL.
It integrates with an existing pre-commit framework if one is present, else installs a native git hook.
Installation is idempotent and clearly reversible (documented uninstall).
verification: go test ./cmd/... (hook installed; blocks on FAIL verdict; idempotent; integrates with existing pre-commit config)
tasks.note: Framework path preferred when .pre-commit-config.yaml exists (its config is COMMITTED, so the whole team gets the backstop; a native hook under .git/ reaches only whoever ran init) — and installing both would run validate twice and fight the tool the repo already chose. Framework entry is `repo: local` + `language: system`, which deliberately avoids depending on a published hook repo (the rowshape GitHub org does not exist yet — see P0-T1) and reuses the binary the user already installed. YAML edited via yaml.Node, not a marshal round-trip, so the user's comments/order/formatting survive (deliberately better than the JSON reformat tradeoff accepted in P3-T8, because .pre-commit-config.yaml is committed and reviewed). Stale entry matched by hook id wherever the user moved it, so an upgrade corrects in place instead of appending a duplicate. Native hook refuses to clobber a pre-commit it did not write (someone else's hook is their whole workflow). Executable bit set — a git hook without it is silently never run. Hook fires ONLY when a migration is staged, matched per detected runner, because validate hydrates a database and paying that on a README typo is how a hook earns its deletion — and a deleted hook catches nothing forever. EXIT-CODE JUDGMENT CALL (documented in docs/mcp.md, worth revisiting): 0 PASS and 2 WARN-only allow; 1 FAIL blocks; exit 3 (tool error, typically Docker down) allows with a loud warning. A tool error is not a verdict; blocking every commit because a daemon is down is what gets the hook deleted or --no-verify aliased permanently, losing the backstop for the FAILs too. A commit is not a deploy and CI still gates the merge. This does NOT touch INV-CONFIDENCE-CAPPING: the hook issues no verdict and cannot turn a WARN into a PASS. TESTING NOTE: the three tests that actually execute the hook were initially gated on runtime.GOOS != windows and SKIPPED locally — i.e. the load-bearing acceptance criterion ('blocks on FAIL') was unverified. Regated on POSIX-shell availability (Git for Windows ships the sh that runs them) and they then FAILED: PATH was set to the stub dir alone, so grep was missing, the staged-migration gate found nothing, and the hook exited 0 before reaching rowshape — meaning the PASS/WARN cases had been passing VACUOUSLY and would have stayed green against a completely broken hook. Fixed the harness (PATH = stubs + the real grep dir), and every exec case now asserts the stub actually ran (RAN_VALIDATE) plus a control case proving the skip-path gate is what skipped. Mutation-tested: pattern-matches-everything and block-on-tool-error each caught. Verified against REAL git: `git commit` with a FAIL stub was blocked (commit absent from log), with an exit-3 stub was allowed with the warning, and with rowshape absent from PATH was allowed.

phases.name: Code-review remediation — blockers, then high, then medium, then low
phases.weeks: 11b (between phase-3 and the phase-4 launch surfaces)
phases.ships: The fixes for every finding from the full-codebase review: two blockers (a blast-radius-zero bypass in `hydrate`, and two analyzers reading unresolved table names as an empty table), five high findings, nine medium, and the low/doc items — plus the test coverage that pins each one so it cannot silently return.
phases.source: Full-codebase review (154 Go files, ~23,870 lines): gofmt/vet/build clean, golangci-lint v2.12.2 zero issues, and the entire suite green INCLUDING every Postgres-backed test, run for real against a disposable local cluster via the README's Docker-free initdb recipe (only TestContainerLifecycle skipped, needing Docker — exactly as the README documents). Findings are correctness/provenance issues, not mechanical ones.
phases.scope_note: This phase is placed BEFORE phase-4 deliberately. Phase 4 is the launch: the GitHub Action, the public docs, and the agent-loop demo that is the week-12 kill-criterion gate. Both blockers are in the code those surfaces render and demo — shipping the launch on top of a `hydrate` that can write to the source host, or analyzers that answer `instant` for a 50M-row table, would publish the defects rather than the product. Nothing here adds product surface; it is correctness, provenance and coverage only. NOTE ON ORDERING: CR-T3 (corpus harness ignores Severity) is promoted above the other high findings because the corpus is the instrument used to verify CR-T2 and CR-T5 — fix the measuring device before trusting its readings.
phases.review_disposition: The review found no defect in the capping ENGINE itself: Ceiling, DependencyConfidence and Cap are correct and well-tested, including a structural test proving a finding cannot self-assert confidence to escape capping. The frozen-package items (CR-T8, CR-T9, CR-T23, CR-T24, in internal/fixture / internal/verdict / internal/toolerror) are missing guards and provenance nits around an otherwise sound design, NOT live bugs — they carry status:blocked pending explicit owner sign-off, because those structs are consumer-facing (npm wrapper, GitHub Action, MCP) and DSSE-signable.
definition_of_done: Both blockers are closed with tests, and each is mutation-tested (reverting the fix turns the new test RED).
Every high and medium finding is either fixed with a pinning test, or explicitly declined with the reason recorded on the story — no finding is closed by silence.
The corpus harness compares severity and rejects unexpected findings, so 'the corpus passes' means what it claims (CR-T3).
Frozen-package stories (CR-T8/T9/T23/T24) are unblocked by owner sign-off or explicitly deferred with a recorded decision — they are NOT silently skipped.
The full suite stays green including the Postgres-backed suites, run for real per the README's initdb recipe; golangci-lint (pinned v2.12.2) stays at zero issues.
No invariant is weakened and no confidence level is downgraded to make any story pass (loop operating_rules).

tasks.title: BLOCKER: `hydrate --target` never checks the host (INV-BLAST-RADIUS-ZERO bypass)
tasks.description: `validate` refuses to run when the target host matches the fixture's declared source (cmd/validate.go:97 calls validate.CheckHost). `hydrate --target <url>` performs the same class of write — CREATE SCHEMA / CREATE TABLE + COPY — and calls CheckHost nowhere: grep across the tree shows exactly one non-test caller, and it is validate. A user pointing --target at the host the fixture was pulled from (an easy mistake, since validate and hydrate share flag names and habits) is silently written into with zero refusal. Compounding it, cmd/hydrate.go has NO test file of any kind, so nothing pins the refusal once added.
acceptance_criteria: loadIntoTarget (cmd/hydrate.go) calls validate.CheckHost(f.Meta.Source, host) before any write, mirroring cmd/validate.go:97, and refuses with the same error surface and exit code.
The refusal covers the equivalence classes CheckHost already handles (verbatim / normalized / loopback aliases) — hydrate must not reintroduce a single-hash compare.
A different host, and 128.0.0.1, are still ALLOWED (the check must not refuse hydrate's legitimate job).
cmd/hydrate_test.go exists and covers both directions: refusal on a matching host, success on a non-matching one.
Mutation check recorded: removing the CheckHost call makes the new refusal test fail.
verification: go test ./cmd/ -run TestHydrate
grep -rn 'CheckHost' cmd/ shows callers in BOTH validate.go and hydrate.go
go test ./...
tasks.note: Closed. loadIntoTarget now calls checkHydrateHost BEFORE anything is created or connected — which had to precede target.NewEphemeral specifically, because NewEphemeral itself issues a CREATE DATABASE on the admin server, so by the time it returns the write to production has already happened. The decision delegates to validate.CheckHost rather than reimplementing it: that function already hashes the target under every spelling that is definitionally the same machine, and the single-hash compare it replaced was a proven bypass — a second implementation here would be a second thing to get wrong. hydrate prints its own refusal wording rather than echoing ErrHostMatchesSource, whose text is validate-specific ('validate: refusing to run...'); the shared thing is the DECISION, not the sentence. cmd/hydrate_test.go is new (the file had zero coverage of any kind): 3 end-to-end tests through runHydrate plus an 11-case table on the predicate, covering both write paths (--target and --ephemeral), the equivalence classes (case, trailing dot, loopback aliases in both directions), --target precedence over --ephemeral, and the negative cases that matter just as much — a different host, 128.0.0.1, and an absent source must all still be ALLOWED, or the refusal stops hydrate doing its job. MUTATION-TESTED, and the first attempt was INVALID: neutering the helper body left the validate import unused, so the build broke, and a build failure is not the same as tests catching the bug. Redone as the true pre-fix state (helper defined, never consulted at the write site), which compiles — both refusal tests then went RED, and the failure output showed hydrate actually reaching the production host: a DNS lookup of prod-db.internal.example.com on the --target path, and 'could not create a disposable database' on the --ephemeral path, i.e. the CREATE DATABASE attempt. SIDE OBSERVATION, feeds CR-T7: that same mutant output leaked `user=admin database=appdb` from a raw pgx error — finding #7 seen live rather than inferred. Verified: gofmt clean, go vet clean, golangci-lint (v2.12.2) 0 issues, full suite green.
tasks.found_by_audit: Full-codebase review. Confirmed by grep: `CheckHost` has exactly one non-test call site (cmd/validate.go:97) and cmd/hydrate*_test.go does not exist. The invariant text and the existing implementation_note in this file describe the refusal as if it guards the product; it guards one of the two commands that write to a target.

tasks.title: BLOCKER: rsperf.go and rsindex.go skip resolveTable — unqualified names read as an empty table
tasks.description: Every other analyzer routes a SQL-extracted table name through resolveTable before looking it up, precisely because an unresolved name yields the ZERO VALUE rather than an error (the bug class already fixed and documented under P2-T8/P2-T9). Counted calls: rslock.go 3, rsdata.go 3, reverse.go 2, rsconstraint.go 1, rsperf.go 0, rsindex.go 0. Consequences: an unqualified `UPDATE accounts SET ...` or `DELETE FROM users ...` (ordinary hand-written SQL — Postgres resolves it via search_path) silently drops RS-PERF-001/002 entirely, and `CREATE INDEX ON orders (...)` silently reports an `instant` duration for what may be a huge table. TRAP FOR THE IMPLEMENTER: the corpus case named `perf-unqualified-update` does NOT cover this. Its 'unqualified' refers to the missing WHERE clause, and its migration uses the QUALIFIED `public.accounts`. Do not read it as existing coverage.
spec_refs: P2-T8 known_defect (the same bug class, already fixed elsewhere)
acceptance_criteria: rsperf.go and rsindex.go route every SQL-derived table name through resolveTable before any fixture lookup, matching the pattern in rslock.go/rsdata.go.
An unqualified `UPDATE accounts SET status = 'active'` against a fixture keyed `public.accounts` produces the SAME RS-PERF finding and the SAME duration bucket as the qualified form.
An unqualified `CREATE INDEX ON orders (...)` produces the same RS-INDEX estimate as the qualified form — not `instant`.
A genuinely unresolvable table (typo / ambiguous across schemas) still refuses to extrapolate rather than reporting `instant`, matching the estimateFor behavior documented under P2-T8.
Tests mirror rslock.go's TestUnqualifiedTableGetsTheSameEstimate for both analyzers.

A new corpus case exercises an unqualified TABLE NAME (distinct from perf-unqualified-update's missing-WHERE meaning), and the misleading name is resolved — either rename that case or document the distinction in its expected.json description.
verification: go test ./internal/findings/ -run 'Unqualified'
go test ./... (with a live PG per README's initdb recipe)
Mutation: removing the resolveTable calls turns the new tests RED
tasks.note:
Closed, and the review's account of it was WRONG IN BOTH DIRECTIONS — recorded here because the corrections matter more than the fix.

UNDERSTATED (rsperf): the review said unqualified DML 'silently drops RS-PERF-001/002'. It does worse. The new corpus case run against the un-fixed analyzer returns `verdict = PASS` on an UPDATE that rewrites every one of 6,000,000 rows — not a WARN with a gap in it, a clean PASS. That is the wrong-PASS this project names unrecoverable (INV-CONFIDENCE-CAPPING), reached without the capping engine being involved at all: capping can only cap findings that EXIST, and this path deleted the finding before capping ever saw it. Worth generalizing — the capping engine is necessary but not sufficient; an analyzer that fails to fire is invisible to it.

OVERSTATED (rsindex): the review said `CREATE INDEX ON orders (...)` 'silently reports instant duration'. It does not. Mutation-tested directly: the unqualified form yields NO bucket at all (""), because P2-T8's follow-up already made estimateFor refuse to extrapolate for a table the fixture does not carry (tableKnown). That path was degraded but fail-safe, and the earlier fix was doing its job. The REAL rsindex defect is one the review did not name and I rate higher: an unqualified CREATE UNIQUE INDEX on a column with PROVEN duplicates (unique=false, exact) was downgraded from severity `error` (this index cannot build) to `info` (uniqueness not confirmed) — a definite build failure softened into a shrug.

FIX: resolution at the callers, parsers left pure, mirroring rslock/rsdata. rsperf resolves in Analyze (cascade target) and in massDMLFinding. rsindex resolves the CREATE INDEX target, and — deliberately — only the REINDEX TABLE name: REINDEX INDEX names an INDEX, which findIndex matches against index names rather than fixture table keys, so resolving it there would be wrong. DependsOn now carries the canonical fixture key, which is strictly more accurate for a DSSE-signed attestation.

TESTS: 5 new (3 rsindex, 2 rsperf + 1 negative), mirroring rslock's TestUnqualifiedTableGetsTheSameEstimate including its guard-the-guard discipline (each asserts the QUALIFIED control is itself alarming, so the comparison cannot pass vacuously). Mutation-tested: all 5 go RED against the pre-fix code; the estimate test logs qualified="outage" unqualified="". A negative test pins that resolution never INVENTS a match — an unknown table still produces no finding.

CORPUS: added perf-unqualified-table-name, verified NON-VACUOUS (against the mutant it fails with 'verdict = PASS, want WARN' + 'missing expected finding RS-PERF'). Did NOT rename perf-unqualified-update: it is referenced in 3 places in rsperf_test.go, and 'unqualified UPDATE' is legitimate SQL phrasing for 'no WHERE' — the name is not wrong, it collides with a different sense of the word. Documented the distinction in both cases' expected.json instead. CAVEAT: that corpus green is weaker than it looks until CR-T3 lands, because the harness still ignores severity and tolerates extra findings; re-confirm there.

Verified: gofmt/vet clean, golangci-lint (v2.12.2) 0 issues, and the FULL suite green against a live PG 18 cluster (README initdb recipe, port 5433) — all 24 packages including corpus/harness 34s, test/action 153s, test/demo 219s.
tasks.found_by_audit: Full-codebase review; confirmed by per-file grep counts of resolveTable across internal/findings/. This is the exact defect recorded as RESOLVED under P2-T8 — resolved for the analyzers that existed then, left unfixed in the two added later (RS-PERF shipped in P5-T1, RS-INDEX in P2-T11).

tasks.title: Corpus harness ignores Severity and tolerates unexpected findings
tasks.description: TestCorpusVerdicts (corpus/harness/harness_test.go:130) compares the overall verdict, then for each EXPECTED finding checks only that its code family is present and that ResolveContains matches. It never compares Severity — even though expected.json declares it and ProducedFinding already carries it (pipeline_test.go:71) — and it never asserts the absence of findings nobody expected. A case therefore passes while an analyzer emits the wrong severity or an extra unrelated finding. ELEVATED ABOVE THE OTHER HIGH ITEMS DELIBERATELY: the corpus is the instrument used to verify CR-T2 and CR-T5, so it should be trustworthy before it is used to sign off on analyzer fixes.
acceptance_criteria: TestCorpusVerdicts compares got.Severity against want.Severity for every expected finding and fails on mismatch.
The test fails when the analyzer produces a finding no expected.json entry accounts for (exact-set comparison, or an explicit allow-list field in expected.json if a case legitimately needs one).
Every existing corpus case still passes, OR a case that only passed because of the gap is fixed and the reason recorded — the fix must not be to loosen the new assertion.
Mutation-tested: flipping one case's produced severity, and injecting one spurious finding, each turn the suite RED.
verification: go test ./corpus/harness/ (with a live PG per the README initdb recipe)
Mutation: perturb a severity and an extra finding; both caught
tasks.note:
Closed, and it paid for itself on the first run by finding two real gaps in the EXISTING corpus.

What was wrong: the assertion loop checked only that each expected finding's code family was PRESENT. ExpectedFinding.Severity was declared in every expected.json and read by nothing; ProducedFinding.Severity was collected by the validator (pipeline_test.go) and then dropped. And nothing looked at findings beyond the expected list, so an extra finding was invisible.

WHAT IT CAUGHT IMMEDIATELY (both legitimate findings that expected.json under-declared, NOT analyzer bugs — checked before declaring them, because papering over a spurious finding would defeat the whole point): (1) reverse-narrow-type also fires RS-LOCK-001(warn) — the migration's OWN COMMENT says 'this also rewrites the table under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, so RS-LOCK fires too', i.e. the author knew and the file said so, but the expectation never declared it because nothing checked. (2) validate_orphans also fires RS-CONSTRAINT-001(warn) — it adds the FK NOT VALID and VALIDATEs it in the SAME transaction, which is RS-CONSTRAINT-001 by definition. Both declared in expected.json with the reason recorded there; neither assertion was loosened. Note (2) also means validate_orphans overlaps rsconstraint-not-valid-same-tx — evidence for CR-T15.

DESIGN: matching is per code FAMILY (what expected.json names), with EXACT per-family counts and severity compared as a MULTISET. Families rather than full codes is deliberate — INV-VERDICT-STABLE keeps codes permanent, but a new sibling like RS-LOCK-002 should not break every case — while exact counts stop an extra finding hiding inside a family it shares. Audited first: every expected finding already declares a severity and no case expects duplicates, so strictness cost zero churn. Added ProducedFinding.FullCode (not matched against, carried so failures name WHICH code arrived).

MUTATION-TESTED both halves: flipping an expected severity gives 'RS-LOCK: severities = [warn], want [info] (produced: RS-LOCK-001(warn))'; injecting a spurious finding gives 'unexpected finding family RS-PERF ...: RS-PERF-999(warn)'. Deleted the now-dead findingByCode rather than suppressing the `unused` lint (P0-T6 discipline).

CONSEQUENCE FOR CR-T2: its corpus case was verified again under the stricter harness, discharging the caveat recorded in that story.

Verified: gofmt/vet clean, golangci-lint 0 issues, corpus + cmd + all internal packages green against live PG 18.
tasks.found_by_audit: Read of TestCorpusVerdicts: the assertion loop covers Code presence and ResolveContains only. Severity is collected into ProducedFinding and then never read.

tasks.title: Calibrate() hardcodes Confidence: measured, escaping the capping rule
tasks.description: internal/estimate/calibrate.go:62 sets `Confidence: string(fixture.Measured)` unconditionally, ignoring the confidence of the row count it projects onto. RFC §7.4 — and models.go's own documentation — require that confidence never exceed the confidence of `rows`. A measured duration extrapolated onto an ESTIMATED row count is not a measured answer. Worse, TestCalibrateUpgradesConfidence currently asserts the hardcode as correct, so the test locks the bug in rather than catching it. Touches INV-CONFIDENCE-CAPPING, the one invariant this file flags as 'must never be wrong'.
acceptance_criteria: Calibrate() caps the emitted confidence at min(measured, confidence-of-rows) rather than hardcoding measured.
A measured duration projected onto an `estimated` row count yields `estimated`, and cannot produce PASS.
A measured duration projected onto an `exact` row count still yields `measured`.
TestCalibrateUpgradesConfidence is corrected (not deleted) to assert the capping rule, and its previous expectation is recorded as the bug it was.
Mutation-tested: restoring the hardcode turns the corrected test RED.
verification: go test ./internal/estimate/
go test ./internal/verdict/ (capping regression suite still green)
go test ./...
tasks.note:
Closed. The defect was worse than 'hardcodes measured': Calibrate did not RECEIVE the row-count confidence at all, so it could not have capped even if it wanted to. The fix is therefore a signature change — Calibrate(op, p1, p2, declaredRows, rowsConfidence) — not a one-line edit.

The correct behavior was already sitting one file away: Extrapolate ends with Min(Estimated, rowsConfidence). Calibrate now ends with Min(Measured, rowsConfidence), which makes the pair symmetric and states the actual rule — calibration RAISES THE CEILING from estimated to measured, it does not bypass the ceiling. The distinction that matters: calibration measures the OPERATION (two real timings establish how the work grows) but evaluates that curve at the DECLARED row count, so a perfectly fitted curve projected onto a pg_stats estimate is still an estimate. Under INV-CONFIDENCE-CAPPING that is the difference between eligible-for-PASS and capped-to-WARN, i.e. the bug manufactured PASS eligibility from a fact that never supported it.

The caller needed no new plumbing: internal/findings/estimate.go already had rowsConf in scope and was passing it to Extrapolate on the very next line — the calibrate branch just never used it.

TEST: TestCalibrateUpgradesConfidence was CORRECTED, not deleted. It had called Calibrate with no confidence input at all and asserted `measured` unconditionally, so it could not have failed however wrong the answer was — it asserted the bug as correct. Its real intent (calibration is the only route to `measured`) is preserved, now with an exact row count. Added TestCalibrateIsCappedByRowsConfidence (5 levels incl. absent, which is any unnamed value and ranks below every named level) and TestCalibrateNeverExceedsExtrapolateOnWeakRows, which pins the relational property rather than the constants: on the same weak row count, calibrating must never outrank extrapolating. Four other call sites in the test file were updated to fixture.Exact, preserving their original `measured` semantics.

MUTATION-TESTED: restoring the hardcode fails 4 subtests with 'rows confidence "estimated" = "measured", want "estimated"'. NOTE this change makes verdicts WEAKER (more capping), which is the safe direction, and the capping regression suite (internal/verdict), findings, corpus and cmd all stay green — nothing depended on the wrong strength.

PROCESS NOTE: the first attempt to write this note corrupted prd.json — backslash-n sequences in a shell heredoc reached the file as real newlines, producing an invalid control character inside a JSON string, and the commit landed before the validation caught it. Fixed by restoring prd.json from the previous commit and re-applying with json.dumps doing the escaping. Build the JSON value with a JSON encoder; do not hand-escape it through a shell.
tasks.found_by_audit: Full-codebase review; confirmed at internal/estimate/calibrate.go:62. Flagged as High rather than Blocker because P2-T14's calibrate path is opt-in (--calibrate), but it is the only place in the tree that writes a confidence level without consulting its dependency.

tasks.title: rsindex parseCreateIndex misreads partial / expression unique indexes as a confident FAIL
tasks.description: parseCreateIndex does not parse WHERE clauses or expression index targets. A legitimate `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... ON t (col) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL` over a column with proven duplicates — the normal soft-delete pattern, where the duplicates live only in soft-deleted rows — gets a confident, wrong FAIL (RS-INDEX-010) instead of passing. A wrong FAIL is less dangerous than a wrong PASS but still breaks the contract, and it is the kind of false positive that gets the tool removed from a pipeline. No corpus case exercises it.
acceptance_criteria: parseCreateIndex detects a partial index (WHERE predicate) and an expression index target.

A unique index whose uniqueness the fixture cannot decide (because the predicate selects a subset no fixture fact covers) produces a capped WARN naming the resolving command — NOT a FAIL and NOT a PASS.
A plain (non-partial, non-expression) unique index over a column with proven duplicates still FAILs with RS-INDEX-010 — the existing true positive is preserved.
A corpus case covers the partial-unique-over-soft-deletes scenario with its expected verdict.
verification: go test ./internal/findings/ -run 'Index'
go test ./corpus/harness/ (live PG)
tasks.note:
Closed. parseCreateIndex now returns a struct (createIndexStmt) rather than a growing positional list, carrying the partial predicate and an expression flag; undecidable() is the single predicate the analyzer branches on.

THE REAL SHAPE OF THE BUG: `unique: {value: false, confidence: exact}` is a proven statement about the WHOLE column. A partial index constrains only the rows its predicate selects, so whole-column duplicates say nothing about it — the soft-delete pattern (CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... WHERE deleted_at IS NULL, duplicates living entirely in soft-deleted rows) builds fine and rowshape called it a confident FAIL. An expression index is the same problem one step removed: a fact about `email` says nothing about `lower(email)`.

WHY IT EMITS WARN EXPLICITLY rather than routing a PASS through capping — this was the subtle part. Capping only downgrades when want==PASS; it reads DependsOn, and the whole-column unique fact is `exact`, so a PASS would have been CERTIFIED for a subset nobody measured. Emitting SeverityWarn gives want==WARN, which Cap leaves untouched: neither FAIL nor PASS is reachable. DependsOn is deliberately EMPTY — citing the whole-column fact would put a false provenance trail into a DSSE-signed document for a conclusion that fact does not support (same concern as CR-T11).

TWO THINGS THE EXISTING TESTS TAUGHT ME, both good catches by the repo's own guards: (1) cmd.TestExplainCoversEmittedCodes enforces that a finding's Remediation CONTAINS the catalog's text verbatim, so finding and `rowshape explain` can never drift. My first version replaced the remediation and broke it; corrected to quote the catalog verbatim and APPEND the index-specific check, which is the seam the test was designed to allow. (2) tools/genfindings.TestFindingsDocsUpToDate requires the committed docs-site catalog to be byte-identical, so the RS-INDEX-010 summary change needed `go run ./tools/genfindings`. Both are exactly the kind of drift guard this codebase is good at.

Also updated the catalog SUMMARY so `rowshape explain RS-INDEX-010` itself explains the partial/expression case — the explain text is a user surface, not just an internal string.

TESTS: 5 new (partial-not-a-FAIL, partial-cannot-PASS, expression-undecidable, plain-unique-still-FAILS as the true-positive guard, and a parser table). Mutation-tested: forcing undecidable()=false reproduces the exact wrong answer — severity `error`, title 'will fail: the column has duplicate values', and a depends_on citing the whole-column fact.

CORPUS: rsindex-partial-unique-soft-delete added and verified NON-VACUOUS (against the mutant: 'verdict = FAIL, want WARN' and 'severities = [error warn], want [warn warn]'). NOTE: CR-T3 caught my own first draft of that case — I declared one finding when the statement legitimately produces two (it is also a non-concurrent CREATE INDEX, so RS-INDEX-001 fires alongside RS-INDEX-010). The stricter harness earning its keep on the very next story.

Verified: gofmt/vet clean, golangci-lint 0 issues, internal + cmd + corpus + tools + fixture-spec all green against live PG 18.

tasks.title: SplitStatementsIn mis-splits E'...' backslash-escaped literals
tasks.description: The statement splitter does not understand Postgres E'' escape-string syntax: it has no backslash handling at all. Verified by direct execution during review — `SELECT E'it\'s; oops';` splits into two bogus statements. The broken half-statement is then fed to capture.Apply, producing a spurious FAIL on a migration that is actually valid. Untested.
acceptance_criteria: SplitStatementsIn treats a backslash escape inside an E'' literal as literal content, so `SELECT E'it\'s; oops';` yields exactly ONE statement.
Standard '' (doubled-quote) escaping, dollar-quoted bodies, and line/block comments continue to split correctly — no regression in the existing cases.
Line numbers reported by SplitStatementsIn remain correct across an E'' literal spanning newlines (the File/Line contract from P2-T7 is load-bearing for P4-T2 annotations).
Table-driven tests cover E'' with an escaped quote, an escaped backslash, and a semicolon inside the literal.
verification: go test ./internal/validate/ -run Split
go test ./...
tasks.note:
Closed. Reproduced first, exactly as reported: `SELECT E'it\'s; oops';` split into TWO statements, the first the broken fragment `SELECT E'it\'s`, which then went to capture.Apply and produced a spurious FAIL on a valid migration.

THE FIX IS NARROWER THAN "HANDLE BACKSLASHES", and that narrowness is the whole point. standard_conforming_strings has been ON by default since PG 9.1, so a backslash in an ORDINARY '...' literal is a plain character. Treating backslash as an escape everywhere would have traded this bug for a new one: `SELECT 'C:\'` would swallow its own closing quote and merge the following statement into it. Verified before writing the fix that `SELECT 'C:\';` currently splits correctly — so that was a behavior to PRESERVE, not a gap. Escape handling is therefore conditional on the E'' prefix, and opensEscapeString additionally requires the E to be a standalone token so the trailing E of an identifier (valueE'...') is not mistaken for one.

Escaped backslash handled explicitly: in E'path\' the \ is an escaped BACKSLASH, so the quote that follows really does close the literal. A naive "skip the character after every backslash" gets this wrong in the opposite direction, which is why it has its own test case.

Also handled doubled quotes ('') explicitly. They previously worked BY ACCIDENT — the scanner closed the literal and immediately reopened it on the second quote, which preserved the text and therefore preserved the split, but only for as long as nothing depended on where the literal actually ended. Costs nothing to state properly.

TESTS: 8 splitter cases plus a line-number test. The line test matters because SplitStatementsIn's File/Line contract (P2-T7) is load-bearing for PR annotations (P4-T2), and an E-string spanning newlines is exactly where a scanner that mis-tracks a literal would drift the line count; it asserts lines 1, 3, 6 across a multi-line E-string.

MUTATION-TESTED IN BOTH DIRECTIONS, which is what this fix needed: reverting to no backslash handling fails the E-string cases; over-correcting to "backslash escapes in every literal" fails the ordinary-literal cases (`'C:\'` and the identifier-E case). A fix that could only be tested in one direction would not have shown that the guard against the naive version works.

PROCESS NOTE: two test-data corruptions during authoring, both from backslashes being eaten in transit through a shell heredoc — once producing an invalid Go escape, once silently turning the doubled-backslash case into a single backslash, which made a CORRECT implementation look like a failure. The escaped-backslash case is now built by string concatenation so it cannot be mangled, and the mutation script mutates by line index rather than by a backslash-bearing text anchor (an earlier mutation attempt silently matched nothing and reported a false green).

Verified: gofmt/vet clean, golangci-lint 0 issues, internal + cmd + corpus + tools green against live PG 18.

tasks.title: validate/hydrate echo raw pgx connect errors (host, port, username) to the user
tasks.description: On a target-connect failure, cmd/validate.go and cmd/hydrate.go surface the raw pgx error into user-facing JSON/stderr, which carries host:port and username. Every other connect-failure path in the tree deliberately substitutes a fixed string for exactly this reason — these two call sites are the outliers. Related precedent already recorded in this file: internal/plan.RedactURL was fixed under P0-T6 for the same class of leak (PRD §5: connection details are never logged or persisted).
acceptance_criteria: Both call sites substitute a fixed, non-revealing message on connect failure, matching the convention used elsewhere in the tree.
The exit code and the verdict/tool-error shape are unchanged (INV-VERDICT-STABLE) — only the human-readable detail changes.
The underlying error is still available for debugging behind an explicit opt-in (e.g. a debug/verbose flag or log level), not on by default.
A test asserts that a connect failure against a URL containing a username and a non-default port leaks neither into stdout nor stderr.
verification: go test ./cmd/ -run 'Connect|Redact'
Manual: run validate against an unreachable host with credentials in the URL; confirm no host/port/user in output
tasks.note:
Closed. Two call sites echoed a wrapped pgx error, which embeds the connection: a real one reads "failed to connect to `user=admin database=appdb`: hostname resolving error: lookup prod-db.internal.example.com". Host, port, username and database name all reached the terminal, the CI log, and --json.

NOT INFERRED FROM READING — seen live. The CR-T1 mutation run printed exactly that string out of hydrate's load path before this story was started; the mutation test here reproduces it again with planted credentials (`user=leakuser database=leakdb`, port 15432).

THE TWO OUTLIERS, both surfacing target.Load's error raw: cmd/hydrate.go used `load failed: %v`, and cmd/validate.go's hydrateApplyEphemeral concatenated err.Error() into a TargetUnavailable tool error. Every other connect-failure path already substituted a fixed string (cmd/pull.go:88, cmd/validate.go:156 and :220 for NewEphemeral and Connect) — this was inconsistency, not an unrecognized risk. Checked asToolError's Internal fallback too: it is NOT reachable with a raw pgx error, because applyAndCapture and hydrateApplyEphemeral wrap every path in a ToolError with a fixed message, so it needed no change.

DETAIL IS GATED, NOT DISCARDED: ROWSHAPE_DEBUG=1 prints the underlying error, and both remediations now say so, otherwise the next person debugging a genuine connection problem is left with nothing. Deliberately an env var and not a flag — the CLI surface is part of the public contract (INV-VERDICT-STABLE), a debugging aid does not belong in it, and an env var cannot leak into a verdict.

TESTS: the leak DSN carries a username, password, non-default port and database name, against a closed port so it fails fast without a live server. Asserted across BOTH streams and, for validate, with --json as well as human output — the machine contract is exactly as public as the terminal. Plus a unit test that the debug opt-in really reveals the error, so the gate cannot silently become a black hole.

MUTATION-TESTED: restoring the raw echo fails with the leak printed verbatim in the test output.

PROCESS NOTE: three separate corruptions in this session from backslashes being eaten in transit through shell heredocs (a Go escape, a test's doubled backslash, and this mutation's newline). Mutation scripts now build escapes via chr(92) and mutate by line index; an earlier heredoc-anchored mutation silently matched NOTHING and reported a false green, which is the failure mode to watch for — a mutation test that does not actually mutate proves nothing and looks identical to success.

tasks.title: FROZEN PKG: render_human fabricates a '0 rows in 0ms' basis for byte-based estimates
tasks.description: internal/verdict/render_human.go prints a '0 rows in 0ms' extrapolation basis when rendering a byte-based estimate (e.g. reindex_bytes). Estimate's numeric fields are not pointers, so 'not applicable' is indistinguishable from 'measured zero' — and the human renderer states the fabricated version as fact on the one line whose job is to say how well the answer is known. INV-DURATIONS-BUCKETS requires the extrapolation basis to be attached and truthful.
acceptance_criteria: A byte-based estimate renders a basis describing bytes, or omits the basis entirely — it never prints a row/duration basis that was not measured.
A genuine measured zero remains distinguishable from 'not applicable' in the human output.

A golden-output test covers the byte-based rendering.

tasks.title: reverse.go dropTableFinding skips resolveTable (wrong provenance on unqualified DROP TABLE)
tasks.description: reverse.go calls resolveTable twice, but dropTableFinding is the one finding in the file that skips it. An unqualified `DROP TABLE users` therefore reports 0 rows and an unresolvable confidence path — wrong evidence and wrong provenance on a document that is DSSE-signable. Same root cause as CR-T2, narrower blast radius, hence Medium.
acceptance_criteria: dropTableFinding routes its table name through resolveTable, matching its two siblings in the same file.
An unqualified DROP TABLE reports the same row evidence and depends_on path as the qualified form.
An unresolvable table still fails safe (capped, not answered from the wrong table).
A test covers the unqualified DROP TABLE case.
verification: go test ./internal/findings/ -run Reverse
go test ./...
tasks.note:
Closed with a one-line change: dropTableFinding now routes its table name through resolveTable, as its two siblings in the same file (dropColumnFinding, narrowTypeFinding) already did.

The consequence was sharper than "wrong provenance". Mutation-tested, the unqualified form produced: "DROP TABLE users is irreversible: all 0 rows are lost" where the qualified form said "all 4.0M rows are lost". So the finding still fired and was still a WARN — but its headline told the user the table was empty, which is the single most reassuring thing it could have said about an irreversible DROP. Evidence rows read 0 and depends_on cited `users.rows`, which resolves to nothing in a DSSE-signed document.

Same root cause and fix as CR-T2; kept separate because the blast radius is genuinely narrower (the verdict does not change) and it belongs to a different analyzer.

Verified: gofmt/vet clean, golangci-lint 0 issues, internal + cmd + corpus + tools green against live PG 18.

tasks.title: rsconstraint checkConflict declares a depends_on fact that does not carry confidence
tasks.description: checkConflict's DependsOn names `table.rows`, but the finding actually rests on the profiled Range fact — which has no confidence field in the fixture schema at all. The result is wrong provenance on a DSSE-signed document: the attestation cites a dependency the conclusion does not use, and the dependency it does use cannot be confidence-checked.
acceptance_criteria: DependsOn names the fact the conclusion actually rests on.
If the Range fact genuinely carries no confidence, the finding is capped accordingly (absent ranks below every level per RFC §7.4) rather than borrowing table.rows' confidence.
Whether Range should carry a confidence field is decided and recorded in docs/DECISIONS.md — changing the fixture schema is an RFC-level decision, not a drive-by edit.
A test pins the emitted depends_on path for this finding.
verification: go test ./internal/findings/ -run Constraint
go test ./internal/verdict/
tasks.note:
Closed. checkConflict now declares `<table>.<column>.range` instead of `<table>.rows` — the fact it actually reads.

WHAT THE FIX EXPOSED, and why it is recorded as a decision rather than patched: that path has no case in verdict.factConfidence, so it resolves to `absent`. That is not a gap to paper over — fixture.Range is {Min, Max, Mean} with NO confidence field at all (RFC §6.1), so there is genuinely nothing to report. Declaring `rows` was borrowing a confidence from a fact the conclusion never touches, which is precisely the wrong answer for a DSSE-signed document.

`absent` ranks below every named level, so a range-based finding can never license a PASS — correct, since a range read from pg_stats is not a proven bound. It does NOT weaken this finding: severity error → want FAIL, and Cap leaves FAIL untouched. Verified by test rather than assumed.

RECORDED AS D-010 in docs/DECISIONS.md with two options and a recommendation: leave Range confidence-less (safe, but a scanned range stays indistinguishable from a planner guess, permanently capping this finding class), or add confidence/via to Range as an additive RFC §12 change (recommended, but as its own RFC revision with a schema bump — not folded into a code-review story). Every other fact in the format carries its confidence, so Range looks like an omission rather than a decision. This also interacts with CR-T28: --exact would have something to upgrade.

Mutation-tested: restoring `<table>.rows` fails on both the path assertion and the "never cites a fact it does not read" assertion.

tasks.title: Runner env injection can emit a duplicate DATABASE_URL
tasks.description: internal/runner/{alembic,prisma,drizzle}.go build the child environment with `append(os.Environ(), "DATABASE_URL=...")`. If the invoking shell already exports DATABASE_URL, the child receives two entries for the same key; which one wins is resolution-order dependent and not something to leave to chance in a tool whose promise is blast-radius zero. Not reachable today (framework-migration capture is not wired into validate yet) — it becomes a live risk the moment it is, which is why it is worth closing before that wiring lands.
acceptance_criteria: A shared helper builds the child env by REPLACING any existing DATABASE_URL rather than appending alongside it.
All three runners use the helper — no per-runner copies of the append pattern remain.
A test asserts that with DATABASE_URL already set in the parent env, the child sees exactly one entry, and it is rowshape's disposable target.
verification: go test ./internal/runner/
grep -rn 'os.Environ()' internal/runner/ shows only the helper
tasks.note:
Closed, but the premise needed correcting FIRST and that correction is the main content of this story.

THE REVIEW'S CLAIM WAS WRONG ON THE MECHANISM. It said a duplicate DATABASE_URL means "which one wins is resolution-order dependent". It is not. os/exec DOCUMENTS the rule: "If Env contains duplicate environment keys, only the last value in the slice for each duplicate key is used." I did not take the doc's word for it either — ran it: with a stale DATABASE_URL exported by the shell and rowshape's appended after it, cmd.Env carried 2 entries and the child process read rowshape's. So `append(os.Environ(), ...)` already selected the disposable target, by a documented guarantee rather than by luck. There was no latent wrong-database bug here, and this story should not be recorded as if there was.

FIXED ANYWAY, for reasons that do not depend on that guarantee: (1) INV-BLAST-RADIUS-ZERO should be legible at the call site — someone asking "can a framework migration reach the developer's real database?" should not have to know a dedup rule inside the standard library to answer it, and the answer should not silently change if this is ever ported to an exec path with no such rule (a container runtime, an SSH invocation); (2) the line was written three times and could drift in one; (3) framework-migration capture is not yet wired into validate, so making it explicit now is free, and after it carries traffic it would not be.

envWithDSN replaces rather than appends, matching case-insensitively because Windows environment variables are case-insensitive and an inherited `Database_Url` is the same variable. rawsql.go deliberately still uses a plain os.Environ(): psql takes its target from argv, not DATABASE_URL, so there is nothing to replace.

TESTS: exactly-one-entry with an inherited value, with none inherited, unrelated vars survive (PATH in particular, or the runner binary is not found), plus a test driving all three runners' real ApplyCmd so a future runner that hand-rolls the append is caught here rather than in review. Mutation-tested: reverting to append gives "got 2: [DATABASE_URL=postgres://stale-from-the-shell/prod DATABASE_URL=postgres://disposable/rowshape]" — which also shows plainly why the old code was survivable, since rowshape's is last.

Verified: gofmt/vet clean, golangci-lint 0 issues, internal + cmd + corpus + tools green against live PG 18.

tasks.title: TestValidateNoCloudEgress scans only two paths, not the transitive closure it claims
tasks.description: cmd/validate_test.go's egress guard scans cmd/validate.go and internal/validate/ only. runValidate actually pulls in runner, target, hydrate and toolerror, none of which are scanned. A future network import in any of them passes the guard silently — and this test is the mechanical proof behind INV-NEVER-GATE-VALIDATE and the privacy claim the docs make verbatim (P4-T4). A guard that names a guarantee it does not actually check is worse than no guard.
acceptance_criteria: The test walks the real transitive import closure of runValidate (e.g. via go/packages or `go list -deps`) rather than a hand-listed set of directories.
A network-capable import (net/http, net, cloud SDKs) anywhere in that closure fails the test.
Legitimate exceptions, if any, are an explicit allow-list with a written reason per entry — not a silent omission.
Mutation-tested: adding a net/http import to internal/runner turns the test RED (this is the case the current test misses).
verification: go test ./cmd/ -run NoCloudEgress
Mutation: add net/http to internal/runner; test must fail
tasks.note:
Closed. The guard now walks the REAL transitive closure via `go list -deps`, seeded from cmd/validate.go's own imports so a newly added dependency is picked up without anyone remembering to extend a list. Coverage went from ~10 hand-listed FILES to 218 PACKAGES.

THE DESIGN QUESTION THE OLD TEST DODGED, and the reason it could not simply be widened: `net` is IN the closure and must stay. validate's entire job is to connect to a Postgres target; pgx opens a TCP socket and resolves DNS to do it. INV-NEVER-GATE-VALIDATE says validate never calls the CLOUD, not that it performs no I/O. A blanket "no networking" rule would be both wrong and unsatisfiable, so the line is drawn at the PROTOCOLS an egress would use (net/http, net/rpc, net/smtp, grpc, the cloud SDKs, and the telemetry clients that are egress by another name — opentelemetry, datadog, sentry). The five legitimately-present network packages are an explicit allow-list with a stated reason each (`net`, net/netip, net/url, internal/nettrace, x/net/dns/dnsmessage), and the test LOGS each one it allows, so the exceptions are visible in CI output rather than buried.

GUARD THE GUARD: the test asserts the closure actually contains internal/target, internal/hydrate, internal/runner and internal/toolerror — the very packages the review found unscanned. Without that, a seeding bug would return a small or empty closure and the test would pass over nothing, which is precisely the failure mode being fixed.

MUTATION-TESTED with the exact case named in the acceptance criteria — adding net/http to internal/runner, which the OLD test could not have caught because it never looked at that package. Now RED, naming net/http/httptrace and three x/net/http* packages.

Note the test shells out to `go list`. That is the same resolver the compiler uses, so the closure cannot drift from what actually gets linked; the cost is that the toolchain must be present, which it is wherever `go test` runs.

tasks.title: FMA fusion is a real threat to the byte-identical determinism claim
tasks.description: internal/hydrate/engine.go and fanout.go contain float expressions of the form `lo + r.float64()*span`. Go's SSA backend is permitted to fuse multiply-add into FMA and DOES so on arm64/ppc64/s390x but not amd64 — producing a different last bit, hence different synthesized values, hence a different output file. INV-DETERMINISM promises byte-identical output on ANY platform, and no cross-architecture test exists to catch a violation. Apple Silicon developers and amd64 CI are exactly the split that makes this reachable.
acceptance_criteria: Float expressions on the synthesis path are written so FMA fusion cannot change the result — e.g. explicit float64() conversion of the product to force rounding, or integer/fixed-point arithmetic on the value path.
A test produces a hydrate output digest and asserts it against a committed golden digest.
The golden digest is verified on at least two architectures (amd64 + arm64) in CI, so a future regression is caught rather than assumed absent.
The rationale is recorded in docs/DECISIONS.md — this is a non-obvious constraint that will otherwise be refactored away.
verification: go test ./internal/hydrate/ -run Determinism
CI matrix runs the digest test on amd64 and arm64 and both match the golden
go build -gcflags=-d=ssa/fma/off comparison (or equivalent) shows no digest change
